even if a guy says hey can you send me photos of your tits you just say simply no you're going to have to wait
a break in rapport anything that actively discourages the deepening or establishment of a positive connection
you should be doing push pulls you should really be doing breaks and rapport or specifically push pulls throughout the entire interaction
the most important part here is to let him initiate a sexual conversation

palm_comparison.vs_us: Outsourced chatting; Palm owns no objection/power-dynamic playbook (§8 OUTSOURCED). Build-toward. Cross-creator consensus (Luca + habibi).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Encode push-pull and let-the-fan-initiate tactics into the in-house chatting SOP rather than leaving tone to the vendor.

consensus_risers.title: Log personal fan details in notes so any chatter can continue the relationship seamlessly

variants.claim: Chatters add personal details (job, location, interests) to each fan's notes or rename field so any chatter who picks up the conversation later can maintain the illusion of a continuous, invested relationship.
variants.what_they_do: After a fan shares personal information in DMs, the chatter immediately records it in that fan's notes inside the OF messaging interface (or an external CRM). The fan's name field may be renamed to include key tags. When a different chatter takes over a shift, they review notes first and reference those personal details naturally — the fan feels remembered and valued, which increases LTV.

variants.claim: Chatters who record personal details shared by fans during conversations can use those details in future sessions to create a sense of genuine ongoing relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to jot down fan-disclosed details — name, age, relationship status, hobbies, personal anecdotes — and reference them in later messages. The explicit goal is to make the fan feel 'seen, heard, and valued' rather than like one of many subscribers.

variants.claim: Building rapport with subscribers requires learning what they are genuinely into (fetishes, hobbies, job) and recording those details in the subscriber's notes field to enable personalized follow-up.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters skip surface-level questions (age, location) and instead probe for fetishes, hobbies, and occupation. Every paying subscriber gets a notes entry with those details so any chatter picking up the conversation later has context. Non-spenders are deprioritized — no time is wasted building rapport with subs who have not spent money.

sources.quote: we'll have our chatting team add notes to each fan where you can write down personable information and the next chatter that hops on to talk to that dude will kind of have an idea
maybe he's told the girl what he does for a living where he lives and we'll put all that information in his notes or in his name
jot down details about them you want to know more than just their name keep track of things like their age relationship status and any personal tibbits they share
what are they into right maybe they what do they do for work what are their Hobbies obviously if we're talking about only fans specifically what are they into like what are their fetishes
you don't want to be able to report with people who are just going to waste your time

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting tools (§9), but no written retention/chatter handoff SOP. The notes-per-fan mechanic described here is a direct operational input for the Fan Tracker and for the in-house chat team SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalise a 'fan card' field set in the Fan Tracker (or OF notes) — at minimum: spending tier, personal details shared, last PPV purchased, last contact date. Require the external chat team to populate this today; make it mandatory in the in-house chat SOP. Aligns with and extends §9 whale-hunting data.

consensus_risers.title: Assign each creator 2–3 content verticals matched to her strengths, then iterate on viral data

variants.claim: Locking a creator into specific content verticals aligned with her strengths makes results predictable and gives the agency a repeatable creative direction model.
variants.what_they_do: For each creator, Ellis identifies her natural strengths and interests, maps them to 2–3 content verticals (e.g. cooking niche with cooking outfits), produces content across those verticals for ~6 weeks, analyses performance data, extracts the viral ingredients, and scales what works. The creator's brief is narrow enough that ideas are abundant and filming is sustainable.

variants.claim: Standardized evergreen content verticals built on the creator's own personality outperform niche-ing or chasing trends.
variants.what_they_do: Plugs every creator into the same proven verticals (e.g. Omegle, talking-head) rather than gym/office/etc niches, posting ~6x/day on IG and ~20x/day on trial reels across 1-2 accounts each; argues 'you are your own niche' and that conversion density (welcome msg, funnel, conversion posts) matters more than raw views.

variants.claim: Running multiple branding angles in parallel in the first two weeks (different captions, framings, positioning — same creator, same account) generates real performance data fast enough to commit to a winner before competitors even finish their first test.
variants.what_they_do: In weeks 1–2 they ran three branding angles simultaneously on the same account (Swedish girl, Ukrainian girl, American girl in Dubai) — same creator, same videos, different captions + framing + marketing positioning. Ukrainian angle got 2.4× the views of the other two combined. They killed the losing angles within two weeks and rebuilt all downstream systems (chatting scripts, DM persona voice, mass messages) to match the winning brand identity within the same week — not a month later.

sources.quote: once you've found the content verticals, it's about trying to turn the creator into the best creator within that space — results then become predictable
We have scientifically engineered specific verticals that work with every avatar fit creator
you are your own niche as a creator
in the first two weeks, we ran three branding angles through trials. We had a Swedish girl, a Ukrainian girl, and the American girl living in Dubai.
the back end just adds on top of it but fast…same week this shouldn't take you a month or two

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline scrapes viral inspo and promotes reels to a creator's board (§1), which is data-driven but inspo-led rather than creator-vertical-led. There is no documented step where a creator is formally assigned 2–3 fixed verticals as a strategic frame before ideation begins.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'vertical assignment' step at creator onboarding: define 2–3 content verticals per creator based on her strengths/interests, then filter inspo scraping (§1 pipeline) to those verticals first. After 6 weeks of posting, run a formal data review to codify which verticals are outperforming, and adjust the inspo source list accordingly.

consensus_risers.title: Brief chatters on model persona: tone, emojis, typing quirks, niche

variants.claim: Chatters must mirror the model's real personality and typing style (excessive letters, cute emojis, pet names) so subscribers cannot detect a VA is behind the keyboard.
variants.what_they_do: The persona section of the training PDF specifies: age range of the model → inferred typing style (e.g. 18–24 = lots of cute emojis, elongated words like 'heyyyy'), the model's content niche, any pet names she uses, and other personality markers. Chatter applies all of these verbatim.

variants.claim: Whales who experience a persona or tone shift at handoff disengage and churn — every chatter must be trained to voice the same persona regardless of which shift they are on.
variants.what_they_do: All chatters are cross-trained on one universal tone and persona voice. Whale interactions are treated as the highest-risk handoff: if Chatter A builds rapport with a whale and Chatter B takes over sounding different, the whale is lost. They explicitly state they have lost 'a few good whales' from this failure. The fix is a documented tone/persona guide that is part of chatter onboarding and ongoing training.

variants.claim: The single highest-impact revenue intervention at $50K+/mo is an audit of chatting: verify mass messages and scripts match the creator's personality sheet, and fire chatters whose pages are underutilized — this caused a same-day revenue spike.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin and Jason logged into Barry's Inflow (chatting platform) as admins and ran a three-part audit: (1) identified what was wrong with chatting mechanics; (2) verified all mass messages and automation scripts correlated with each creator's personality sheet; (3) identified and fired unnecessary chatters — reducing from 6 to 4 — freeing the remaining chatters to work larger, more active pages. Revenue spiked the same day.

sources.quote: take note of the client's Persona so if the girl is aged between say 18 to 24 then using a lot of cute emojis when typing using a lot of excessive letters in some words
they all need to be cross-trained and they need to talk in the same persona. There's one universal tone on how to talk to people, especially whales
we went through all your mass messages we first identified what was wrong with your chatting and we looked at the correlation between all your personality sheets of your clients with the chatting
firing unnecessary chatters because you had like six chatters at the time when you really only needed four

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) and there is no documented persona-briefing process internally. When Palm builds in-house or AI chatting, voice consistency will be critical — especially for AI personas (§3) where breaking character destroys the illusion.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'chatter voice card' field to the Creators table in Airtable (and AI Account Profile for AI personas). Populate: age, typing quirks, signature emojis, pet names, niche. Feed this card to any chatter (human or AI) at session start. Especially high priority for AI persona chatting.

consensus_risers.title: Train chatters with agency-owned PPV scripts rather than relying on their defaults

variants.claim: Not all chatters come with polished PPV skills; agencies that hand chatters specific scripts and strategies outperform those that leave chatters to their own methods.
variants.what_they_do: Black Cherry operates a chatting team internally but also licenses their PPV script framework to other agency operators. They provide PDF script playbooks, documented PPV funnels, and course-based training covering at least seven distinct PPV strategies. The agency owns the IP; chatters execute to spec.

variants.claim: Sending a structured, zipped training module to new chatter VAs — including a persona guide, payment info, and scripts — accelerates onboarding and ensures consistency.
variants.what_they_do: When hiring a chatter VA, Bjorn compiles a ZIP package containing: (1) a PDF guide on the model's persona (age range, niche, typing style, pet names, emojis), (2) payment/platform instructions, (3) a pre-written script. The VA unzips and self-trains before starting.

variants.claim: Chatting drives 80%+ of OF revenue; chatters must have scripts with specific photos, videos, and voice notes pre-loaded so they can send PPV content in real time without hunting the vault mid-conversation.
variants.what_they_do: Agency builds a script library where each conversation branch has the relevant media asset already tagged/attached. Chatters follow the script and send the pre-matched content without interruption, maintaining flow and urgency in the conversation.

sources.quote: you need to know these strategies so that you can train shatters to be this good not all Chatters have mastered the game they know what they know but when you can give them scripts like this
Down Below there's actually going to be a PDF attached where you can see a very precise example of how we run this

some training material that I prepare for our VAs so when I hire a VA for the purposes of chatting I'll input the necessary information from the model in this guide here zip it up along with a few other things and then send it to the VA the chatter as a training module

80% or more should come from the chatting the messages and the tips so because that's the most important part you must have a system in place so you must have set scripts that you or if you've hired somebody to the to do the chatting they can follow now Within These scripts they should have pre-made content already there so photos and videos maybe even some voice notes

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced with no internal scripts or SOPs (§8). When Palm builds its in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap), it will need proprietary script libraries and training material. This competitor model — agency owns the playbook, chatters execute — is the architecture Palm should adopt.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Begin building Palm's internal PPV script library now, drawing from competitor frameworks like this one. Structure it as: (1) welcome/intro message template, (2) conversation bait posts, (3) upsell ladder scripts per price tier, (4) objection-handling / repackaging scripts. This becomes the training corpus for both the in-house chat team and the AI chatting project.

consensus_risers.title: Build backend capacity ahead of creator pipeline, not reactively after signing

variants.claim: An agency must have more operational infrastructure than its current client load requires at all times, so any inbound creator can be onboarded immediately without dropping balls on existing clients.
variants.what_they_do: The speaker frames this as a readiness posture: 'if I know that any given week there might be a creator that reaches out that I'm really interested in, I need to have more infrastructure than needed for my current client base.' Reactive hiring after signing = delayed onboarding, overwhelmed ops, damaged existing accounts, and lost creator trust. The prescription is to treat hiring/staffing as a proactive capacity investment, not a response to demand.

variants.claim: An unsystemised OF backend (no tracking links, no per-creator content plan, inconsistent handling) will collapse under sudden high sub volume, wasting the traffic wins.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis describes an incident where a creator brought in 3,500 subs overnight and the agency had to pull chatters from other models, scramble chat managers, and operate chaotically. His prescribed fix: implement tracking links per traffic source, standardise how every creator's page is managed (content plan, PPV plan, data review), and ensure every process has an SOP so any new chatter/manager can be slotted in without handholding.

variants.claim: Agency infrastructure — dashboards, chatter management hub, and content distribution channels — must be fully configured before onboarding the first creator so signing can be fast and error-free.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin uses: Notion for the agency dashboard and KPI tracking; Discord as the chatter management server (chatters plugged into one repeatable process as they're hired); Telegram for content distribution. All three are set up in the foundation phase, before any outreach begins.

sources.quote: you actually need to have a backend infrastructure ready to take on more profiles before those profiles even are coming to you
you have no tracking mechanism — you're probably not using tracking links to see where the traffic's coming from, and every single creator is getting handled in a different way
get your notion dashboard set up, your team management, your Discord hub for chatting, your Telegram for content distribution. Everything needs to be set up before even signing a creator
manage these chatters in Discord from day one. That way when you hire more and more, it's just plugging them into the same process

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led and opportunistic (§6). There's no documented ops-readiness check before signing. The onboarding wizard captures creator info (§6) but does not gate signing on whether Palm has chatter/VA/posting capacity available. As Palm scales, this gap becomes a compounding risk — each new signing without backend capacity degrades existing creator performance.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a pre-signing ops checklist to Palm's creator onboarding flow: before progressing a prospect to contract, confirm available chatter slots (external team capacity check), Publer/Amin posting bandwidth, and editor queue load. If capacity is at limit, flag for Josh to hire/expand before signing. This is a lightweight systems fix (§systems) that prevents the self-destruction loop the video describes.

consensus_risers.title: Revenue-gated hiring ladder: VAs, then chat manager, then ops

variants.claim: Hires should be added on a revenue-gated ladder — owner does everything to ~5k, VAs/chatters at 5-20k, managers at 20k+, ops director at 50-100k+.
variants.what_they_do: 0-5k owner solo; 5-20k first VA/chatter (after chatting yourself 2 weeks); 20-40k chat manager first then content/account managers; 50-100k+ ops director to execute proven systems so the owner exits day-to-day.

variants.claim: Agencies that scale successfully add team members and formalize systems incrementally as revenue grows (every $10k step), rather than brute-forcing revenue and attempting to retrofit structure later.
variants.what_they_do: TDM describes a stepwise pattern: $10k → $20k adds first team members; $20k → $30k formalizes contracts; $30k → $40k introduces KPI tracking; $40k → $50k and onward continues compounding systems. The key is that structure is built in proportion to the revenue step, not deferred.

variants.claim: A practical delegation trigger for a horizontal agency is one account manager hired for every 10–20 creators, focused purely on client communication and routine tasks.
variants.what_they_do: Hunter's explicit hiring cadence: start solo, outsource chatting/content to a chatting agency immediately, hire first internal person when workload is unmanageable (focused on creator comms and daily duties), then scale at 1 hire per 10–20 creators. At 16 creators with in-house chatting, he calculates ~48 chatters (3 per creator) plus 4+ managers for oversight pods of 10.

sources.quote: you need to hire managers
10 turned into 20 but a couple team members came on board. 20 turned into 30 which meant that you started to build out proper contracts.
Once I surpass the 10 model mark, I'm going to start hiring one person for every 10 models to keep up with the client communication and all the duties that they need to take on.
You need about 48 people that you need to manage. Of those 48 people, you're going to need pods of 10 with management.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's org is thin with no documented hiring criteria, comp bands, or scaling plan (§10). This is a ready-made staffing/comp ladder.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Use this ladder as a template for Palm's org-scaling plan; map Palm roles (Amin, editors, chat) onto the stages and define revenue gates for next hires.

consensus_risers.topic: Minimum viable operating system / weekly scorecard
consensus_risers.title: Run the business on a 4-part minimum viable operating system anchored by a weekly scorecard

variants.claim: A simple four-part operating system — weekly scorecard, creator profitability tracker, outcome-based roles, and founder replacement ladder — is sufficient to escape the $100k/month trap without building a bloated corporate structure.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's weekly scorecard covers four domains: (1) Revenue: creator gross, agency net, gross profit, owner profit, revenue per creator, top/declining performers, content compliance; (2) Acquisition: outreach sent, replies, qualified leads, booked calls, shows, closes, new creators signed; (3) Delivery: content posted, PPV performance, rebuilds, expired fans, chat performance, creator complaints; (4) Team: who hit targets, who missed, which bottleneck repeated, what was fixed. The delivery operating system specifically needs 7 pieces: weekly creator review, content output tracker, chat performance tracker, revenue breakdown, issues/bottleneck list, next week's action plan, and a clear owner for each action. The founder replacement ladder moves any recurring founder task through: do it → document it → delegate it → review the output → metrics manage it → someone else fully owns it.

variants.claim: A fixed weekly operating cadence plus enforced standards (recognition and consequences) keeps an agency from regressing into reactive firefighting.
variants.what_they_do: Installs a Monday hour-one reset (look back, look forward, pick 1-3 priorities, schedule, overflow), a weekly single 'game tape' root-cause review, decision-only compressed 30-min meetings, and a recognition/consequence system; 'culture is what you tolerate and what you reinforce.'

variants.claim: A scaled agency runs on connected central databases (accounts, team, content, finance) so operational detail lives in systems, not the owner's head.
variants.what_they_do: Runs six linked Notion systems (model account manager with logins/warmup SOPs, VA/team tracker with pay+KPIs, content HQ, finance tracker, iPhone tracker, AI to-do list) that interlink so the owner only reviews and decides.

sources.quote: you literally just need a very simple minimum viable operating system with four parts. A weekly scorecard, a creator profitability tracker, outcome-based roles, and the founder replacement ladder

A simple delivery operating system needs seven pieces. A weekly creator review, a content output tracker, a chat performance tracker, a revenue breakdown, an issues and bottleneck list, and next week's action plan, and a clear owner for each action.
you can't scale what you only feel. You scale what you can see every week.
Without rhythm, everything will regress into chaos
Culture is what you tolerate and culture is what you reinforce
the model account manager

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has strong content pipeline instrumentation (§1) and invoicing automation (§7), but no weekly business-level scorecard covering all four domains, no delivery operating system for creator accounts, and no documented founder replacement ladder (§10 GAP, §9 GAP). The SMM hub redesign (§11) addresses social content visibility but not the business-health scorecard layer Dylan describes.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a lightweight 'Agency Weekly Scorecard' page in the admin portal (or a recurring Airtable view) covering all four scorecard domains. Prioritize delivery tracking first (content compliance + chat performance + revenue per creator) since that's where Palm currently has the most instrumentation gaps. The founder replacement ladder should be applied immediately to Evan and Josh's recurring tasks — document and delegate at least one recurring task per week. This is a direct complement to Palm's in-flight SMM hub consolidation (§11).

consensus_risers.title: Classify every creator A/B/C and allocate resources accordingly — cut C creators

variants.claim: Most agencies carry too many low-quality ('C') creators out of fear of losing gross revenue, which silently caps growth by stealing attention from better creators.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan classifies creators into three tiers: A = high upside, consistent content, coachable, scalable brand; B = potential, needs structure, worth developing; C = inconsistent, ignores feedback, creates chaos, low upside, drains team. A creators get premium attention and resources. B creators get active development. C creators get fixed, downgraded, or removed. The diagnostic question is: 'Knowing what I know now, would I sign this creator again today?' If no, there must be a reason she is still consuming company resources. He notes a 30k/month agency with 2 great creators beats a 100k/month agency with 14 nightmares.

variants.claim: If one creator leaving cuts agency revenue by 30–40% or more, the agency is not a real business — it is gambling, and concentration in a single creator is the most common structural failure at the $20–50K/mo level.
variants.what_they_do: Renato uses a live coaching-call example: a client with ~18 models had his top model leave the day before a call, immediately wiping out the majority of his net profit. Renato's prescription is to structure the agency so no single creator departure is catastrophic — achieved through (a) owning traffic assets, (b) salary-model deals, (c) enough creator diversification, and (d) a replicable onboarding/growth system so any creator can be replaced quickly.

sources.quote: the creator audit is where you classify your roster into A, B, and C
knowing what I know now, would I sign this creator again today? That is the last question. That's very important.
most agencies carry way too many Creators, and they keep them out of the fear of losing their revenue number
his main client left him. And she — I think he has around 18 models and she was his main model. And she left the day before our call. And so most of his net profit and revenue just went away
if one model leaving cuts your revenue in half or 40% or a lot, your agency isn't structured in the correct way.
Systematize everything. Have a structure. If you don't, if one model leaves, you're fucked. That is not a good agency structure.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a `Creators` table with status tracking and onboarding workflow (§6), but no documented creator-tier classification, no systematic review cadence, and no written policy on when to downgrade or drop a creator. This is a GAP (§9 retention, §7 pricing/deal structure). Palm's creator acquisition is currently reactive/Josh-DM-led (§6) with no quality filter documented.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement a quarterly creator audit in the admin portal: list all active creators, assign A/B/C rating based on gross profit, content compliance rate, team hours consumed, and a 'would sign again?' flag. Set explicit policies: A creators get priority chat team allocation and content pipeline slots; B creators get a 60-day improvement plan; C creators get a downgrade notice or off-boarding. Pair with the profitability scorecard (finding above) to make ratings data-driven rather than subjective.

consensus_risers.title: Multi-stage application filter: attention check + AP score + template effort scoring

variants.claim: Embedding a small compliance instruction in the job post (e.g. 'write I understand at the bottom') combined with checking onlinejobs.ph Application Points and requiring a filled template eliminates ~40–50% of low-quality applicants before any interview.
variants.what_they_do: Three-layer pre-screen: (1) embed a hidden instruction in the job post requiring a specific phrase to prove they read it; (2) on onlinejobs.ph, prioritize applicants who spent more Application Points (higher AP = higher motivation, lower AP = mass-applying); (3) send all applicants a structured template (name, age, experience, English level, open-ended questions like 'describe your communication style') and filter out anyone who answers lazily or skips fields.

variants.claim: A slow, multi-stage hiring funnel with strict filtering criteria is cheaper than hiring the wrong person, whose true cost in lost time, training, and mistakes runs into tens of thousands of dollars.
variants.what_they_do: Agency runs a dedicated hiring funnel lasting roughly one week: job post → multiple filtering stages → skills/personality tests → pick only the single best-qualified applicant. If no applicant clears the bar, the owner does the job themselves for another 2 weeks and re-runs the funnel rather than settling. Once hired, the employee gets a fully thought-through onboarding, individual KPI tracking, and formal performance reviews.

variants.claim: Replacing ad-hoc 'hire and hope' with a structured vetting process — Google Form application, defined training SOPs, and clear role expectations — results in hires who are productive from day one.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin's system uses Google Forms for initial applicant screening (filtering for baseline competence and fit before any call), paired with written training SOPs so hired VAs/chatters know exactly what to do from day one without hand-holding. Barry specifically credits a VA hired through this system as being immediately effective. The process is described as straightforward but most operators never implement it.

sources.quote: put a simple instruction next to the job post. So, if you apply, text me I understand at the bottom of your application
you always want to look at the AP... if there's an application with just one AP, one application point, means he probably sent it to like a thousand other agencies
they should fill out a template sent in the back to you with like the name, the age, job experience, English level, and just a couple of questions
set up a direct hiring funnel that may even take like a week to get all the applicants

the VA we hired through the system she knew exactly what she was doing from since day one

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter hiring baseline (§8 outsourced). This is pure build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat team roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build these three filters into Palm's future chatter hiring SOP as Stage 1. The AP-score filter is onlinejobs.ph-specific and low-effort; include it as a default screen. Design the template with at least one open-ended question specifically to measure effort, not content.

consensus_risers.topic: PPV price escalation ladders by fan tier
consensus_risers.title: Tiered PPV price escalation: 50% steps for low spenders, 70% steps for high spenders; break $200 cap via tip + multi-unlock

variants.claim: PPV prices should escalate by a set percentage each step within a loop, calibrated to the fan's spending tier, and the $200 OnlyFans cap is not a real ceiling — it can be bypassed via tips or multi-PPV unlocks.
variants.what_they_do: Low spenders (max purchase <$50): increase price ~50% per step. Example ladder: $35 → $55 → $85 → $125. High spenders (max purchase >$120): increase ~70% per step. Example ladder: $50 → $85 → $150 → $255. To exceed the $200 OF cap: (a) request a tip first, then send the PPV, or (b) split into multiple PPVs (e.g. 4×$200 = $800 total) framed as 'unlock these now and I'll send the main video while I get it from my archive.' Always frame multi-unlocks as a favor/bonus. If a subscriber accepts a dropped price too easily, immediately add more PPVs to the bundle to recover revenue ('I had an idea — because it's you, I want to throw in some extra stuff').

variants.claim: Setting price per fan from their purchase history, backing off after a no and pushing higher for proven whales, beats one fixed price for all.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard uses dynamic pricing read from each subscriber's unlock history via tools like Infloww: if a fan stalled at $65 he offers 17/27/47/59 next time, but if a fan will pay $150-$200 for what others pay $20 for he sells at that willingness, and he keeps explicit content premium-priced so perceived value stays high.

sources.quote: for low spenders, which I would define as someone with a maximum purchase of $50, we can increase the price each time by about 50%
for higher spenders, subscribers with a maximum purchase of over $120, we can try and increase the price by about 70% each time
you can't send a pay-per-view over $200. Sure, you can't, but you can still get around that in other ways
you pick a different selling strategy for every single subscriber
the low price might make him not want to buy instead of buy... keep the content premium priced

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model or escalation framework (§7 GAP — explicitly called out as the #1 creator concern). This is directly applicable intelligence for building Palm's pricing playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document these two ladders (50% / 70% escalation by tier) as Palm's default PPV pricing framework. Add the $200-bypass mechanics (tip-first, multi-unlock bundle) to chatter SOPs. Critically, also adopt the 'add more content rather than discount' rule for objection handling — this protects price integrity over time and should be a standing chatter policy.

consensus_risers.topic: OF vault folder structure for chatter efficiency
consensus_risers.title: Organise OF vault into named script folders so chatters can locate PPV assets instantly

variants.claim: Structuring the OF content vault into named folders (e.g. 'Script 1', 'New Subscriber', 'PPV', 'SFW Feed') tied to specific chat scripts reduces chatter errors and speeds up PPV delivery during live conversations.
variants.what_they_do: Inside the OF vault the agency creates dedicated folders for: (1) each reusable sexting script (Script 1, Script 2, etc.) containing all media needed for that session; (2) a 'New Subscriber' folder with content specifically used in the new-sub welcome sequence; (3) a general 'PPV' folder; (4) optionally an 'SFW' folder for feed reposts. When a chatter replays a script with a new subscriber they navigate to the matching script folder to pull the correct assets in order.

variants.claim: A disorganized vault and generic hotkeys are the single largest hidden tax on chatter productivity; restructuring vault with categories and building custom per-creator hotkeys raised reply rates 60% in two weeks.
variants.what_they_do: Vault restructured within week 1: every content piece labeled by category (boy/girl, tier list, JOI, rapport building), exact price per video, and a defined script order (first PPV → second PPV → optional upsell → conditional branches). Chatters open CreatorHero, see the script order, copy-paste, send — 50-second reply time, no scrolling. Hotkeys are custom per creator: matched to the creator's speaking style, age, aesthetic, and specific vault content. Separate hotkeys for small talk, transitions, objection handling. The 18-year-old blonde's hotkeys use youth lingo; the milf's don't. Result: chatters spend 80% less time searching, 80% more time selling.

sources.quote: if you have big sexting scripts...you'll create folders in the vault called like script one script two Etc which is where all the content relating to that script will go
the easier you make it for them the less chance of mistakes there will be
we label everything. categories, scripts, boy, girl, tier list, jerk off instructions, rapport building, every single pricing for the video, and the script order
we build custom hotkeys per creator, per script, and they are specific to the way she speaks, the way she looks
our reply rate went up by like 60% in the first two weeks just from this

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's OF vault/PPV content system is THIN (§2) and chatting is currently outsourced (§8). There is no documented vault folder convention or script-to-asset mapping. This is directly relevant to Palm's roadmap item of building an in-house chat team and AI chatting system.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As build-toward for the in-house chat team: design the OF vault structure around scripts rather than raw chronology. Create a folder schema (New Subscriber, Script_01…Script_N, PPV_unlabelled, SFW_Feed) before handing off to chatters. Document which assets belong to which script in Airtable so the AI chatting system can programmatically select the right media during a conversation.

consensus_risers.topic: Client wins database as retention and sales ammo
consensus_risers.title: Maintain a running 'wins database' — screenshots, metrics, clips — per client for retention and resigning

variants.claim: Systematically capturing and storing client wins (lead screenshots, view counts, clips) in a tagged database gives the CSM live ammunition to counter 'are you doing anything for me?' objections, improves retention at renewal, and provides case study material for new sales.
variants.what_they_do: Notion 'wins' database with fields: win type (leads/views/revenue/video performance), client relation, screenshot or media URL, date. Anyone on the team can add a win. KPI: minimum X wins per client per month set as a CSM metric. Wins roll up to client OS so the CSM can open a client record and instantly see a count and list of wins. Used on retention calls, renewals, and sales calls. Goal: ~20+ wins/month company-wide as a tracked number on the client success dashboard.

variants.claim: Agencies should run a standing mechanism that captures every creator win, screenshot, and testimonial as it happens, since proof fuels both acquisition and trust.
variants.what_they_do: Routes every positive creator message, analytics screenshot, and voice note into a Drive/table immediately, so each win can later be turned into an ad or case study; recommends installing this even before running ads.

sources.quote: we want to create a culture at our company that we collect wins, we celebrate wins, and we stack wins. We have almost a thousand wins of just screenshots
I would try to get like at least a couple of wins a month for each client and make that like your KPI internally from like a client success perspective
when you're on a call with a client and they're like, 'Oh, I'm just not really seeing anything.' It's like, 'Dude, like we've gotten you 10 leads.'
start installing the systems to capture proof like immediately
you need to like start having a system within your agency that captures proof

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has whale-hunting and Fan Tracker tools (§9) for fan-level data, but no documented system for capturing and storing creator wins (viral posts, subscriber milestones, revenue records) as a retention tool. The §9 gap also notes no written retention strategy. A wins database is directly applicable: Palm's editors and social team observe creator wins daily and could log them systematically.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'Creator Wins' table in Airtable (Content Ops or HQ base) with fields: creator, win type (milestone sub count, viral reel, PPV revenue record, press mention), media URL, date. Make logging a win a required step for editors/social team when they observe a notable metric. Josh or a creator success role reviews and shares wins back to creators weekly — this doubles as creator retention messaging and provides case study content for acquisition.

consensus_risers.title: Every team member must own a single measurable number — vague roles produce vague performance

variants.claim: Any metric that no one on the team explicitly owns will default back to the founder, making him the bottleneck and preventing scale.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan assigns a specific owned number to every role: setter owns qualified booked calls; closer owns cash collected; chatter manager owns chat revenue and quality; account manager owns creator retention and content compliance; creative director owns post volume and content performance; ops manager owns scorecard completion and weekly execution rhythm. He uses a 5-level accountability ladder: (1) wait to be told, (2) ask what to do, (3) recommend and act, (4) act then inform, (5) full ownership / exceptions only. Chatter managers should be at level 3-4; ops/sales managers at level 5. The coaching method to move someone from level 2→3: 'Before you come to me with a problem, bring me the problem, three options, and your recommendation.'

variants.claim: A commission-only base combined with milestone bonuses, daily ranked leaderboards shared with the team, and regular chat reviews sustains chatter performance over time and is the structural reason revenue grew from ~$20k/month to $80k+.
variants.what_they_do: Daily: an assistant compiles each chatter's individual revenue and ranks them on a leaderboard shared with the whole team — publicly feeding ego and competitive instinct. Comp structure: commission only plus bonuses tied to achievable milestones (the presenter spent three days designing this mathematically; result was a ~25% revenue lift). Chat reviews: manager logs into accounts periodically, reviews a sample of conversations, records a screen-capture with verbal feedback, sends to the chatter. Every chat review correlates with a numbers increase. Pre-training filter used before formal training to screen candidates — only those who pass pre-training get to the actual training stage. Hiring criteria codified into a checklist (not all personality types are suited to the role).

sources.quote: Every person on your team should own a number because any number nobody owns defaults back to you
Level three, recommend act. These people, they come to you and say, here's the problem. Here's the options. Here's my recommendation. Do you agree?
the setter owns qualified calls, the closer owns cash collected, the chatter manager owns chat revenue and quality
every single day I have my assistant compile a report of the rankings of all of the salespeople because I give out bonuses
my compensation and bonus structure which I believe is key to the continued motivation of my chatters… numbers have gone up about 25% as a result

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has defined Clerk roles (super_admin, admin, editor, ai_editor, chat_manager, creator) but these are access-control roles, not outcome-ownership definitions (§10 GAP). There are no documented KPIs, scorecards, or accountability levels per role. The chat team is outsourced (§8), so the chatter manager role isn't yet internal, but the account manager, editor, and Amin (manual posting) roles have no documented owned numbers.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For each internal Palm role, define one owned number and the accountability level it should operate at: editor → content posted on schedule + revision rate (level 3-4); Amin/posting contractor → on-time post compliance rate (level 3); future chat manager → PPV conversion rate + response time (level 4-5). Add these to a simple weekly scorecard reviewed by Josh/Evan. This is foundational infrastructure before Palm scales headcount further (§10 GAP).

consensus_risers.topic: Outreach message framing — authority + personalization, no direct pitch
consensus_risers.title: Open with a conversion-problem hook; never pitch the agency in the first DM

variants.claim: The first DM should establish authority by identifying a specific problem in the creator's setup rather than pitching the agency, and should funnel toward a no-pressure discovery call rather than explaining the revenue split.
variants.what_they_do: Template opener: 'Hey [Name], I came across your socials across a few platforms and noticed a few things in your setup that might quietly be hurting conversions.' Then a brief authority statement. Never mention the split in DMs — save that for the call. On the call: ask current earnings, show what's possible, then close on next steps and send a contract via Dropbox.

variants.claim: The opening DM should be short, reference one specific problem visible on the creator's profile (e.g., wrong link-in-bio, no reels), and pique curiosity — never a long copy-paste paragraph.
variants.what_they_do: Agents identify a concrete, observable weakness on the creator's profile before messaging, then write a brief DM that names that problem implicitly or asks a curiosity-driving question. No feature-dumping or price-quoting in the first message. Outreach is sent from a personal IG account even with as few as 150 followers.

variants.claim: Structure the first cold DM to intrigue, spark curiosity, and end with a clear call-to-action, then move to a call instead of selling in DMs.
variants.what_they_do: ICC framework: first 4-5 visible words must intrigue (no 'hey hope you're having a great day'), then say something that piques curiosity, then a CTA (reply / send number / book a call). Don't treat DMs as a Q&A; get to the point and get them on a call. Type every message manually, personalize lightly, space sends (~20-30/hr, ~400-500/day across many accounts), and follow up daily for up to 2 weeks ('the money is in the follow-up').

sources.quote: Hey Name, I came across your socials across a few platforms and noticed a few things in your setup that might quietly be hurting conversions
Don't explain the split on the DMs. Obviously, explain the split on the actual call itself
focus on the problem that specific model has. So this could be the lack of posting reels, the wrong link in bio
I stands for intrigue. C... stands for curiosity and the next C stands for call to action.
what you're trying to do is get their phone number and call them as soon as possible.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented outreach message template or sales-call funnel (§6 GAP). The split is set per-creator at onboarding but no pre-call qualifying script exists (§7 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Draft a 2–3 message DM sequence template: (1) problem-hook opener with authority, (2) 24-hr follow-up voice note, (3) call-to-action to book a 10–15 min discovery call. Create a call script with qualifying questions (current earnings, goals) before revealing the split. Store both in the acquisition runbook.

consensus_risers.title: A defined niche, not looks or tech, separates earners

variants.claim: The biggest driver of five-figure outcomes is a hyper-specific model niche selling a defined fantasy and connection, not production quality or attractiveness.
variants.what_they_do: Reject 'basic blonde' generic models; build niche verticals (nurse, air-hostess, alt/gamer, ethnicity, trans) so 'fans feel she's for them', raising perceived value and scarcity; enforce that the niche/branding stays consistent across bio, highlights, reels, names and the Fanvue vault.

variants.claim: Choosing a specific non-adult hobby niche for a creator (e.g., fishing, golf) allows social media algorithms to categorize and distribute content to an engaged audience, dramatically increasing viral reach compared to generic dance/lifestyle posts.
variants.what_they_do: Agency identifies a real hobby the creator has, then builds all social content around that niche. The algorithm can categorize the content, show it to an existing interested community, and that community's engagement triggers viral distribution. Paid shoutouts on niche pages also become cheaper and more targeted. Creator generates content organically during activities she already does.

variants.claim: Choose content by giving each creator a defined niche and building around her individual strengths, not by copying others' viral clips bar-for-bar.
variants.what_they_do: Calls 'no niche' the #1 agency mistake; with 4M+ creators you must stand out. For each creator, identify strengths/weaknesses (some are confident on camera, some shy-but-flexible) and create content that is easy for her to replicate and doesn't feel like work, rather than blindly recreating another creator's viral reel.

sources.quote: It's the niche
you're selling connection and fantasy
if you find a niche for your model, that will make the social media's job a lot easier to find the target audience
within literally one or two seconds I can see exactly what's wrong, which is there is no niche.
you must understand what are their strengths and weaknesses and play into their strengths. create content that is simple for them to do.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's inspo pipeline (§1 MATURE) decides what to film from viral data but is not framed around per-creator niche/fantasy positioning; positioning is implicit, not codified.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add an explicit niche/positioning step to the inspo pipeline for real creators and enforce cross-platform branding consistency; high-value overlap with current work.

consensus_risers.topic: Content category taxonomy for OF vs social
consensus_risers.title: Use a five-category taxonomy to separate OF feed, PPV, SFW social, dating-app, and Reddit-specific content

variants.claim: Explicitly separating content into distinct category buckets (Kinks, Lingerie, Nude/NSFW, Reddit-specific, Social Media SFW) prevents giving away premium content for free on the OF feed and ensures the right content reaches the right platform.
variants.what_they_do: Five named folders per creator: (1) Kinks — fetish-specific; (2) Lingerie — semi-explicit, suitable for OF feed teaser (avoids giving away too much); (3) Nude NSFW — for sale only, not posted free; (4) Reddit — platform-specific content including niche/kink subreddit variants (e.g. pixelated content for specific nerd subreddits); (5) Social Media SFW — safe-for-work used on IG, Twitter, dating apps. The agency explicitly states it does not recommend giving away nude content for free.

variants.claim: Tagging each image file with short codes (e.g. RS, RN, T, PPV) for every platform it has appeared on prevents accidental reuse and lets chatters/managers instantly see which assets are still available for a given platform.
variants.what_they_do: Each uploaded image is numbered sequentially and given a suffix string of platform codes: RS = Reddit selfie subreddit, RN = Reddit niche/kink subreddit, T = Tinder/Bumble, PPV = uploaded to OF vault. A legend document defines all codes. When selecting content for a new post the manager scans the suffix — an image showing only 'T' has never been used on Reddit and is free to post there. Once used, the new code is appended. Content is pre-sorted into category folders: Kinks, Lingerie, Nude/NSFW, Reddit-specific, Social Media (SFW).

variants.claim: Organizing vault content by year/quarter/month/week with descriptive labels enables fast retrieval and reselling to new subscribers.
variants.what_they_do: Creator maintains a Dropbox folder tree (Year → Q1-Q4 → Month → Week → Day), names individual files descriptively (e.g. 'pink outfit 1', 'pink outfit 2'), and mirrors this structure inside the OF vault using OF's native label/folder feature. Only edited, reusable content is saved. Legacy content is batched into a single 'pre-2022' folder and backfilled gradually rather than all at once.

sources.quote: you've Got The Kinks then you got lingerie which is applicable for any not safe for work kind of setting like only fans particularly the only fans feed because you don't want to give away too much
Reddit gets its own folder because subreddits require verifications you'll have to create content that is specific to subreddits on Reddit
you number them all one two whatever and then there's a letter for each instance it's been used on that platform

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's pipeline (§1) categorises content through the 8-step inspo flow and separates social from OFTV (§2), but there is no documented five-bucket content taxonomy distinguishing OF feed teasers from PPV vault content from Reddit-specific variants from SFW social. The social vs OF vault split is structural but the sub-categories within each are not defined.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt or adapt a formal content taxonomy in Airtable's Assets table. At minimum add a 'Content Category' single-select field with values: SFW-Social, Lingerie-Feed, NSFW-PPV, Reddit-Specific, Kink-Fetish. This enables filtering, chatter-facing views, and ensures editors and chatters never accidentally schedule premium content to the free feed.

consensus_risers.topic: Three non-negotiable reel criteria: aesthetics, emotional curve, WTF moment
consensus_risers.title: Reject any reel missing aesthetics + emotional curve + pattern-interrupt — reshoot it

variants.claim: The difference between 6K-view and 6M-view reels is an engineered three-part formula: matching aesthetics to creator USP, a scripted look-away→look-in→smile emotional arc, and a deliberate stop-scroll pattern-interrupt moment.
variants.what_they_do: Aesthetics: outfit, location, lighting, framing all matched to the creator's visual USP (e.g., if USP is large chest + black hair, 80% of content angles highlight that — not hidden in hoodies). Emotional curve: reels are scripted with a 3-beat arc — look away (living her life) → look in (she noticed you) → smile (girlfriend moment) — engineered to create GFE in 3 seconds, repeated across the ~46 views a typical fan sees before subscribing. WTF moment: every reel has a deliberate background event (someone walking into a pole, a cat crossing) that breaks autopilot scrolling and triggers rewatches, which signals the algorithm.

variants.claim: Each platform rewards a specific content format; matching format to platform algorithm dramatically increases reach and watch time for AI model accounts.
variants.what_they_do: Instagram: long-caption 'storytelling' posts where the persona narrates a relatable story (ex-drama etc.) — increases watch time which triggers broader algorithmic push; captions generated via ChatGPT from real OFI model examples. Also 'caption hook videos' with an on-screen text hook. TikTok: slideshow format prioritized because TikTok is actively promoting slides and they go viral more easily; targets lonely-men content angles. Threads: short punchy text posts ('somebody FaceTime me') that feel real and prompt engagement from the target demographic.

sources.quote: Look away says she's living her life. She's not looking at you. Look in says she just noticed you watching. Then smile.
the emotional curve is the precise difference between a clip that gets 6,000 views and a clip that hits 6 million views
every reel has a stop scroll moment…something in the background, something natural, something the viewer didn't expect
Find some good storytelling post from OFI models on IG and use chat GTP to make your own
when it comes to Tik Tok you're going to want to do more slides because Tik Tok really favors slides

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's inspo pipeline (§1) identifies *what* to film from viral data but does not codify *how* to film it at this granularity. The emotional curve and WTF moment criteria are absent from Palm's current documented production standards. Palm's editor workflow has approval gates but no stated rejection criteria at this level.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add these three criteria as a mandatory editor QA checklist in Palm's Task/approval workflow. For aesthetics: add a 'creator USP card' to each creator's Airtable record (visual USP, don'ts list) that editors reference on every clip review. For emotional curve and WTF moment: create a one-page shoot brief template with examples that goes to creators/videographers before every filming session. Clips failing any criterion should be flagged for reshoot, not edited around.

consensus_risers.title: Hard floor of 50/50 revenue split — never go lower

variants.claim: Agencies should refuse any deal below a 50% commission split, framing it as the clean, fair baseline rather than a negotiable starting point.
variants.what_they_do: States a non-negotiable 50/50 split as the minimum: if a creator earns $2K/month, the agency takes $1K. No tiered structures, performance bonuses, or sliding scales are mentioned — flat 50% is presented as the standard.

variants.claim: 50/50 is the standard baseline commission split for new OFM agencies, with room to take a higher percentage as expertise and results grow.
variants.what_they_do: Agency locks in 50/50 as the minimum split with every creator. As the agency proves its value and techniques mature, it renegotiates upward. No tiered structure described — purely experience-based progression.

variants.claim: Charge around 50% for full-service management rather than discounting to a low percentage to win clients.
variants.what_they_do: Treats 50/50 as industry standard for doing everything (marketing + chatting); explicitly warns beginners against charging 20-30% out of desperation. Nuance: when starting with sub-$1k creators, stay around 40-60% (default 50%); higher 30-40% can fit only specific larger cases. Logic: getting a creator 1k->10k is harder than 37k->120k, so don't give away margin on the hard early work.

sources.quote: don't go below 50/50 revenue split. If your model makes $2,000 a month, that's 1,000 for her and 1,000 for you. Clean, fair, and simple.
you say let's partner up, I'll take 50%, you take 50%, this is the benchmark, this is the baseline
charging like 20, 30, 35% when you're just starting out... the industry standard, go with 50% and you can't go wrong with that.
a 50/50 deal is quite common in the only fans management industry.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per creator at onboarding but has no documented split rationale, floor, or tier model (§7 GAP). The commission field exists in the Creators table but the logic behind what percentage to set is unwritten.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's commission policy explicitly: establish a floor (50% is industry-standard per this and other sources), define whether Palm ever takes less (e.g., for a high-revenue creator where 50% would be disproportionate) and codify the rationale. Add this to the creator onboarding wizard as a displayed policy, not just a blank field.

consensus_risers.topic: Free teaser content as pre-sell warm-up
consensus_risers.title: Send 1–2 free teaser images before any PPV offer to prime the subscriber

variants.claim: Sending free 'outfit' or soft-tease images before the first paid offer raises arousal and reduces friction on the first purchase, making the entire escalation ladder more likely to convert.
variants.what_they_do: At the start of each scripted session, the chatter sends 1–2 free images — typically a suggestive but non-explicit 'outfit' shot — with no price attached, framed as casual sharing ('what do you think about this outfit?'). Only after the subscriber reacts positively does the chatter pivot to the paid ladder. This gates the sell behind a micro-engagement that confirms interest and builds investment.

variants.claim: Starting PPV with a free non-revealing image, then a small $5–15 paid image, then escalating to $30–40+ video creates a progressive upsell funnel that qualifies spenders before committing to expensive content.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter sends (1) a free, non-revealing image to open the conversation; (2) a slightly more revealing image/clip priced $5–15 to qualify whether the fan will spend; (3) subsequent escalating PPV pieces priced $30–40+. Welcome/intro message is auto-sent at $15–25 on subscribe. Each step is treated as a 'drink at the bar' — the fan self-selects into a higher spend tier organically.

sources.quote: I'll maybe send you a couple pictures just for free no price attached of my outfit maybe I look cute I'm like yeah what do you think about this outfit
ideally it's something sexual that makes the subscriber horny and he's like okay yeah that's hot... now after that you can be like yo do you want me to show a bit more
the next video or picture will be a little more revealing you'll sell that for five to ten dollars and so on and so forth the next one from there will be 30 to 40
the first ppv messages should be pictures that are like five to ten dollars you're just trying to qualify if this guy is a spender or a Time waster

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chat SOP exists at Palm (§8). This specific 'free teaser before first pitch' mechanic is a discrete, concrete tactic to encode into the future chatter SOP and AI chatting state machine.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a mandatory 'warm-up phase' in the chat SOP: 1 free teaser image sent before any PPV offer, followed by a reaction-check message. Only proceed to the $15 entry PPV after a positive subscriber response. This applies directly to AI chatting — build the state machine so it cannot enter 'sell mode' before completing the warm-up phase.

consensus_risers.title: Never send mass PPVs — individual messaging generates 3-4x more revenue

variants.claim: Mass PPVs lose far more money than they make because the non-buyers (majority) could have been converted through individual, personalized outreach.
variants.what_they_do: They avoid mass PPV blasts entirely. Instead, chatters message each subscriber individually, open with small talk to warm them up, then present content as if it was created specifically for that fan. The argument: of 1,000 subscribers who receive a $20 mass PPV, ~50 buy; but of the 950 who didn't, 100–200 more could be closed through 1-on-1 conversation — revenue that is permanently lost with mass sends.

variants.claim: Sending PPV messages at minimum once daily, using a TV-advertising repetition mindset, is the single biggest lever for DM revenue most creators underuse.
variants.what_they_do: Creator sends PPV mass messages at least once per day, sometimes multiple times. Frames it explicitly as a repetition/awareness play (Tide detergent analogy): fans who don't buy message #1 may buy message #5. Explicitly rejects the 'one message per week' approach. Pairs this with multiple free posts per day on the OF feed to maintain presence.

variants.claim: Using OF's native list feature to segment fans by content preference (feet, striptease, etc.) and renewal status enables personalized PPV sends that feel direct and increase conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Creates named lists inside OF (beyond defaults): VIP list for high-paying subscribers ($200/mo tier who get PPV free), preference-based lists (feet fans, striptease fans, etc.). When sending a PPV mass message, selects the relevant list as the target or excludes VIP list from paid messages. Sends preference-matched messages to niche lists with personalized copy to increase perceived intimacy.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting (§8) and has no internal SOP on PPV delivery method. No documented position on mass vs. individual PPV exists. This is pure build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat team roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat SOP, institute a hard policy against mass PPV blasts. Default all PPV delivery to individual chat sequences. Use this framing in chatter training: mass PPV = lazy; individual = 10x revenue floor.

consensus_risers.title: Write immersive, boner-test PPV descriptions that sell the fantasy before the sub sees the content

variants.claim: Long, vivid PPV descriptions that pass a 'can you picture it with your eyes closed' imagination test and a 'does it arouse' boner test dramatically increase unlock rates because the sub is buying stimulation from the description itself, not the preview.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are required to write out full descriptions for every PPV — not 'here's a pic of my boobs babe' but a multi-sentence, immersive description of camera angle, positioning, what the model is doing, and what the sub should imagine. Two quality tests: (1) Imagination test — can someone visualize exactly what is in the content just from the words, with no preview? (2) Boner test — does the description itself arouse? If a male chatter reads it and finds it hot, it will likely work. Pre-writing descriptions for an entire set before the selling session begins is standard practice. This is treated as a core skill, not an afterthought.

variants.claim: Framing PPV offers as time-limited and personally exclusive ('only for you') reliably increases conversion rates.
variants.what_they_do: Two distinct psychological levers are scripted: (1) urgency — 'only available for a few hours'; (2) exclusivity — 'I don't usually send this to just anyone but you're special.' Both are delivered conversationally so they feel organic, not like a sales blast.

sources.quote: they are not vivid they do not stimulate the subscriber's imagination
does the description vividly describe what is in the content… will the description give the sub a boner when they read it
make the offer feel exclusive and time sensitive example I have something special for you but it's only available for a few hours
I don't usually send this to just anyone but you're special I love talking to you

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no internal PPV copywriting standard (§2 THIN, §7 GAP). This is directly actionable: even before building an in-house chat team, Palm could create a PPV description template and writing guide for the external chat team and for creator-facing content briefing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a PPV description writing guide with the two-test framework (imagination + boner test) and worked examples contrasting bad ('here's a pic babe') vs. good (fully immersive). Deliver this to the external chat team now as a quick win. When building the in-house team, make PPV description writing a standalone scored exercise in the pre-training filter — candidates who can't write a vivid description after coaching should not progress.

consensus_risers.title: Use an 'innocent yes-ladder' question to psychologically obligate the fan before sending a PPV

variants.claim: Asking 'Can I film a video for you, but I'm a bit shy — do you promise to go easy on me?' gets a yes-commitment that makes the fan feel obliged to purchase when the PPV arrives.
variants.what_they_do: Before sending any PPV, the chatter asks a softly framed question that (a) gets a 'yes' and (b) maintains the model's innocent persona. The two components are explicit: 'can I film for you' = the commitment; 'I'm a bit shy' = innocence framing. Once the fan agrees, the PPV is sent. The felt obligation drives conversion.

variants.claim: Setting up a PPV in advance — by mentioning a dream, fantasy, new outfit, or story — gives chatters a credible, personalized reason for sending the video later, making it seem far more valuable than a cold send.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter plants an anchor in DMs (e.g. 'I just had the craziest dream about doing XYZ with you in my green lingerie') or on the feed (e.g. 'just got this new outfit, what do you think?' with a photo). They do NOT sell immediately. They return 6, 12, or 24 hours later referencing the anchor ('I couldn't help but make that dream a reality — I put on that green lingerie and…') and send the PPV. The delay is what makes it feel authentic rather than scripted. Feed-level anchors (outfit post, emotional story post) can hit many subs simultaneously, with chatters following up individually in DMs.

sources.quote: Can I film a video for you, but I'm a bit shy? Do you promise you will go easy on me?
he feels obliged that he has to buy it because he already said yes to filming the video
we can set up pay-per-views in advanced in the DMs or on the main feed... this should act as lore for the pay-per-view, which gives you a reason for sending it later
that delay between when you bring up the anchor and when you go into sell it is what makes it seem authentic

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline (§8, outsourced). This is a specific pre-sale conversion mechanic not present in any Palm SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Incorporate a scripted yes-ladder template into the in-house chatter playbook as the mandatory bridge between rapport and first PPV send. A/B test the exact phrasing ('can I film for you' vs. 'I made something for you') and track conversion rate per chatter.

consensus_risers.title: PPV Looping: Follow up within 5–10 seconds of any purchase to chain sales

variants.claim: The best time to sell a subscriber another PPV is immediately after they make any purchase, capitalizing on the 'f*** it' impulse to keep the buying momentum going.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters monitor their CRM (e.g. Infloww) for any purchase notification — old PPV, mass PPV, feed post unlock — and follow up within 5–10 seconds with a curiosity-hook message before the fan even finishes watching what they bought. The follow-up doesn't reference the old video; it teases the next thing ('I was literally just about to do something I don't usually do'). From that reply, the chatter plans to sell 5 sequential PPVs, each progressively more explicit or longer, so each one appears more valuable and justifies a higher price. This loop restarts every time the fan buys.

variants.claim: After the first PPV purchase, cycling between descriptive text exchange, free teasers, and the next PPV sell maximizes session revenue; stopping the moment the fan finishes or runs out of money protects the long-term relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Post-first-purchase loop: (1) ask 'what would you do now if you saw me like this?' to get descriptive text, (2) mirror his fantasy back, (3) insert free teaser (photo/voice/short clip — not enough to satisfy), (4) set up next PPV with yes-ladder, (5) sell. Repeat until fan orgasms or explicitly says no money. At that point, immediately pivot to relationship-building mode — pre-recorded 'had a great time' appreciation video, romantic post-session questions.

sources.quote: the best time to sell something to a subscriber is right after they've made a purchase
chatters know the second they get that notification on their inflow or whatever CRM you use, that they need to follow up with the subscriber instantly
descriptive text, sound turned on, get descriptive text from him, make him imagine the scenario in his head, send free teasers, set up the next PPV, and sell the PPV
Sell until you can sell, right? Until he's tapped out or he comes.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is fully outsourced (§8). There is no internal SOP, chatter workflow, or CRM trigger process documented. This is a concrete, mechanics-level system that would form the core of Palm's future in-house chat team playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, make PPV looping the foundational selling framework. Define the CRM trigger (purchase notification), set a hard response-time SLA of <30 seconds, write curiosity-hook templates for the follow-up message, and require chatters to plan a 5-PPV ladder before entering any loop. Build this into chatter onboarding SOPs.

consensus_risers.title: Three-stage follow-up protocol for unopened/unpurchased PPVs — guilt-trip only as last resort

variants.claim: When a fan doesn't buy, a structured follow-up sequence (curiosity ping → reason discovery → resolution → guilt-trip last resort) recovers sales without burning the relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Stage 1 — curiosity ping if left on seen: 'Where did you go?' / 'Don't just stare at it, you're making me nervous.' Stage 2 — if he replies but won't buy, ask why (trust issue, no money, bad timing) and resolve the specific objection (offer AI verification tip, suggest waiting). Stage 3 — only if he refuses to explain and is likely a time-waster: guilt-trip ('you made me film it and I'm not getting anything').

variants.claim: Sending a lone sad-face emoji to a subscriber who has gone quiet triggers guilt and reliably provokes a response, re-opening the monetization window.
variants.what_they_do: When a subscriber has not replied to a previous message, instead of sending follow-up texts or more content pitches, the chatter sends only a '😢' or '🥺' emoji. Bjorn claims ~99.9% response rate. The mechanism: guilt-trips the subscriber into explaining their absence, which restarts rapport and spending. No spamming — one emoji only.

variants.claim: Sending multiple unanswered messages in a row appears overwhelming and spammy, actively pushing subscribers away and reducing future engagement.
variants.what_they_do: The VA training guide explicitly prohibits sending additional messages if a subscriber has not responded. One message sits unanswered → chatter holds. The only permitted follow-up is the sad-face emoji tactic (single message). This is a firm SOP rule, not a suggestion.

sources.quote: If he left you on scene, you want to follow up, but don't be negative yet. Don't guilt trip. Use that as the last resort.
you want to find out why he isn't buying and resolve it

if a subscriber has not replied to your previous message and you want to get his attention simply just send a sad face emoji it is amazing how this will work I would say this probably works 99.9% of the time

do not spam subscribers with messages if they're not responding try not to send additional messages if the sub is not responding as this can appear overwhelming and spammy and could potentially put the subscriber off

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline (§8, outsourced). Palm has no documented follow-up or objection-handling flow.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build this three-stage ladder into the in-house chatter SOP with explicit scripts for each stage. Flag time-wasters in the Fan Tracker (§9) after Stage 3 failure so chatters deprioritize them per the subscriber-priority queue (see finding on emoji tagging below).

consensus_risers.topic: Loom-to-SOP pipeline for building a scalable SOP library
consensus_risers.title: Record a Loom, paste the auto-transcript into ChatGPT, and get a step-by-step SOP in minutes

variants.claim: The fastest way to build an SOP library is to record a Loom of yourself doing the task, copy the auto-generated transcript into ChatGPT with a 'turn this into a step-by-step SOP' prompt, and paste the result directly into a categorized Notion SOP database.
variants.what_they_do: Loom.com recording → copy transcript → ChatGPT prompt → formatted SOP pasted into Notion SOP library, tagged by department (sales, client success, hiring, organic marketing, etc.) and status (draft/active). SOPs are linked from daily winning formula tasks and role onboarding courses via hyperlinks. Cultural rule: when a team member asks a question, don't answer it directly — create an SOP around it, send the SOP, and store it. The SOP library is filtered into role-specific views so each team member sees only their relevant SOPs.

variants.claim: Recording yourself doing each task as a Loom video (rather than written docs) is the most reliable way to train hires and remove misinterpretation.
variants.what_they_do: Films a Loom once per task and reuses it forever; pairs Loom with backup documents for objections; uses live chatter monitoring on first 1-2 shifts plus 'trust but verify' checks for the first 1-2 weeks.

sources.quote: what we like to do is we like to use a tool called Loom, loom.com, and we just film a video on what we're doing. You go to the video, go to transcript, copy the transcript, turn this into a SOP
anytime somebody asks a question, what we're not doing is we're not just going to answer the question right from the jump. We're just going to create an SOP around it
these are all the SOPs. This is sales, client success, hiring, etc.
Loom recordings

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented SOP library (§10 gap). The content pipeline (§1) is highly systematized in code and CLAUDE.md but not in a human-readable SOP format accessible to editors or Amin. The Loom-to-SOP method is extremely low-friction and matches Palm's current AI-assisted workflow culture.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Start Palm's SOP library with three highest-leverage SOPs: (1) editor submission-to-approval workflow, (2) Amin Telegram posting protocol, (3) creator content filming brief format. Use Loom + ChatGPT as described. Store in Airtable or a Notion page linked from the admin portal. Target: 10 SOPs in 2 weeks, then assign new SOPs as a recurring task whenever a process question is asked more than once.

consensus_risers.title: Treat the vault as inventory: weekly script refresh and analytics pruning

variants.claim: Top operators run a weekly operating system that constantly adds new PPV scripts and prunes underperformers by analytics so whales never exhaust the content.
variants.what_they_do: Weekly: add 1-2 new sexting scripts, generate fresh wall teasers, log what content/price performs, replace underperformers (scripts that cause unsubs), keep the vault on-niche and price-test ranges — because a whale burns through 3-4 scripts fast and an empty vault leaves money on the table.

variants.claim: The most effective chat scripts are built bottom-up from live testing — chatters freestyle, track what gets positive responses and high PPV buy rates, and promote those lines/setups into the official script iteratively.
variants.what_they_do: The agency does not hand chatters a finished script to copy-paste. They provide the structural framework (rapport → passive tease → yes train → PPV ladder → gratification close) and then chatters freestyle within it. When a specific line or PPV setup consistently produces positive fan responses or high buy rates, it gets promoted into the agency's canonical script. The script is therefore a living document refined by actual conversion data.

sources.quote: You need to always stay on top of refreshing your scripts
a high quality vault from day one
If we see that a certain sentence has positive feedback from most subscribers, we will use it in the script. If a PPV has a high buy rate with a certain PPV setup, we will use it in the script
how we create scripts in our agency is we get on the page, we start chatting and see what works

palm_comparison.vs_us: OF vault/PPV is Palm's thinnest area with no system (§2 THIN); fan/retention analytics is partial (§9). No documented vault-as-inventory refresh loop.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Spec a vault-inventory cadence (weekly script adds + analytics-driven pruning + price testing) for the future AI line and in-house chat/vault build, wired to fan analytics (§9).

consensus_risers.title: Score every creator on gross profit, team hours, and operational drag

variants.claim: A creator profitability scorecard — covering direct costs, team hours, founder attention, growth potential, and content consistency — is the core tool that separates good business decisions from emotional guesses.
variants.what_they_do: For each creator Dylan tracks: gross revenue, agency net, direct costs (chat team, editor/VA, software, promo), gross profit, team hours required, founder hours required, growth potential, churn risk, content consistency, and operational drag. He illustrates that a creator doing $12k gross with $1k costs and zero management friction (producing $5k profit) can be more valuable than a $20k creator costing $4k in fulfillment (producing $6k profit) but consuming disproportionate attention. The scorecard feeds an A/B/C creator classification used to allocate resources and make cut decisions.

variants.claim: A single visual Miro board showing all employees with ratings, target revenue, timeline progress, current revenue, and per-creator ratios makes every bottleneck visible and creates a clear step-by-step operating orientation for the agency owner.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin builds and shares a Miro board with each student/client. The board contains: all team members with a performance rating next to each, target revenue goal, timeline to hit it, current revenue, and per-model revenue ratios. It is described as 'basic tracking' — not pulling granular IG KPIs — but the visual format makes bottlenecks immediately apparent. It is credited as the primary driver of Barry's jump from $80K to $100K/mo.

sources.quote: this is why you need a creator profitability scorecard. For every creator, the score is gross revenue, agency net, direct costs, gross profit, team hours required, founder involvement required, growth potential, churn risk, content consistency, and operational drag

Creator B does 12K gross. You keep 6K, but she's easy to manage. She sends content like a clockwork. She barely touches your attention. And the team cost against her is only 1K. So she produces 5K in profit.
