you built this entire like visual map of my agency that I've never seen in that way every bottleneck was just visible right there on the screen
we have every single one of your employees with a rating next to it the target revenue the progress to the timeline your current revenue your ratios for each model

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has whale-hunting and Fan Tracker CRM on the fan side (§9), and invoicing computes agency net per creator (§7), but there is no per-creator profitability scorecard that includes direct fulfillment costs and operational drag. This is an explicit GAP (§7). The Airtable `Creators` table exists but is not instrumented for margin tracking.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'Creator Profitability' view to the admin portal (or Airtable `Creators` table) with fields: gross revenue, agency net, estimated fulfillment cost (chat allocation + editor hours + VA hours + tools), gross profit, estimated team hours/week, estimated founder touch/week, ABC rating, and a 'would sign again?' flag. Run the audit monthly. This directly addresses Palm's §7 GAP and feeds the retention/churn work (§9).

consensus_risers.title: Reddit at scale (500+ posts/day) requires a full-time in-house developer — it cannot be outsourced or off-the-shelf

variants.claim: Posting at scale on Reddit (hundreds of posts per day across many accounts) is impossible to do manually and requires custom-built automation maintained by a full-time developer who is embedded in the team, because Reddit continuously changes its systems and breaks off-the-shelf tools.
variants.what_they_do: Agency employs a full-time developer whose primary job is building and maintaining Reddit automation: account creation pipelines, karma farming bots, posting schedulers, and proxy/anti-detect management. When Reddit changes its platform, the developer patches the systems. Currently runs ~2 full-time humans monitoring and managing captions/targeting on top of the automation layer. Reduced headcount from ~20+ contractors to ~6 fixed contractors by automating the posting layer.

variants.claim: A $100K/month promo machine requires two distinct ops roles: one that expands the contact network daily and one that schedules, tracks, and enforces timing on every active promotion deal.
variants.what_they_do: Network getters are in Telegram groups every day adding new creator and agency contacts. Queue managers hold the schedule of every live S4S and GG deal, confirm partners post on time, create unique tracking links per promo, and run profit analysis to identify high-ROI partners for reinvestment. A separate media buying team handles paid IG meme-page buys. Reddit/X contractors distribute content as an additional traffic stream. All roles are concurrent and ongoing, not launch-only.

sources.quote: posting 20 posts from 100 accounts every day. That's 2,000 posts a day. That's not something a VA can do… you need to automate things like that
you are basically forced to hire a developer… He needs to be part of the team
your network getters are reaching out to new leads and creators every day expanding the size of your network
your queue managers are constantly in combo with all of your network arranging shout out for shout out and GGs

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has Evan as tech/product (§10) and an existing Apify + Next.js + Airtable stack (§0). Palm could theoretically build Reddit automation on top of existing infra, but it would represent a significant new development investment. Palm has no Reddit automation currently (§5 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Do not attempt Reddit at scale without dedicated developer capacity. Palm should treat Reddit automation as a distinct engineering project, not a VA task. If/when Reddit is added to Palm's traffic stack and proves ROI at the single-account level, scope a Reddit automation module as a separate build-plan item. Use the 500-posts/day benchmark as the threshold at which manual VA posting becomes unscalable and automation investment is justified.

consensus_risers.title: Hold weekly video calls with each creator to give account updates and collect feedback

variants.claim: A recurring weekly Zoom/video call with the model — showing results, flagging problems honestly, and requesting content adjustments — is the primary relationship-maintenance mechanism.
variants.what_they_do: They schedule a standing weekly call per model on a video platform (Zoom or equivalent). The call covers: current account performance update (good and bad news), requested content changes for the coming week, and any operational issues. The visual face-to-face element is cited as relationship-strengthening beyond what async messaging achieves.

variants.claim: Sending a net promoter score survey to all clients on the first of every month, routing responses into a tracked database, and requiring the CSM to respond to every score — including passives and detractors — directly improves retention and provides data to improve the product.
variants.what_they_do: Notion form (NPS survey) with name, email, score (0–10), and open comment. Repeating task on the 1st of each month assigned to CSM with the form link and a pre-written email template embedded in the task. On submission: Notion automation sends internal team email alert and a thank-you email to the client. Scores roll up to the client OS (average NPS per client visible at a glance). CSM board shows unresponded scores filtered by 'responded = unchecked.' Company-wide average NPS tracked on client success dashboard. Threshold: any score below 8 triggers immediate action review.

sources.quote: set up a Cadence of calls like on a weekly basis where you get on a zoom or get on some sort of call with the model you see them face to face and you explain what's going on
NPS. It's called net promoter score. Detractors, passives, promoters. We want to set up a system where we can send this out once per month
we don't want somebody to fill out an NPS form and then we don't respond
if this is below 8, below 9, I need to focus on this. I need to like dial this in like really really hardcore

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator success cadence or QBR rhythm (§9 flags this as a gap). Communication is through Telegram per §5. There is no standing weekly call structure with creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement a standing weekly 20-min creator check-in call. Build a lightweight call template in Notion: (1) last-week metrics summary pulled from OF dashboard, (2) content requests for next week, (3) any platform/account issues, (4) open Q&A. Log call notes back to the Creators Airtable record. This directly fills the §9 retention/churn gap.

consensus_risers.topic: Fan connection before subscription as LTV driver
consensus_risers.title: Build parasocial fan connection via social persona BEFORE the OF subscribe to raise LTV

variants.claim: Fans who feel connected to a creator's persona — trusting her, finding her funny, being attracted to her personality — before they subscribe spend more and stay longer, measurably raising LTV 20–30% vs. content-first pages.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin's agency builds a distinct social persona for each creator so fans are 'invested before they even spend money.' The persona is expressed across IG/TikTok (top-of-funnel), and the brand promise is consistent with what fans find on OF. This is tracked: they observe LTV and CVR lift of 20–30% after implementing persona-first strategy.

variants.claim: Sending a warm, smiling 'thank you' video after a fan's spending session — when he is emotionally most vulnerable — builds the relationship bond that drives repeat monetization rather than churn.
variants.what_they_do: When a fan signals spending fatigue, the chatter stops pushing PPVs entirely. Instead, the model sends a pre-filmed 'gratification video': short clip of her smiling, saying she had a great time, expressing genuine-seeming warmth. The chatter then attempts to extend the chat slightly rather than letting the fan leave immediately. The logic: the fan is emotionally open at this moment; a positive close imprints a good memory that makes him return for the full PPV ladder again next session.

sources.quote: if you can create that connection before the only fans like they're invested already before they even spend money that's what creates retention
I've seen my LTV go up like 20 30% along with CVR along with all these other metrics

this is just a simple video of the model saying thank you and that she had a really good time to the camera being happy smiling. So you make the fan feel good. This is where they're most vulnerable to relationship building
you repeat this process until the fan can't spend anymore. When the fan can't spend anymore, you don't want to force any more sales because this will just ruin the whole relationship

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline (§1) is strong on viral content distribution but the baseline doc does not document a persona-consistency check between social identity and OF page experience. Fan retention strategy (§9) tracks whales and going-cold signals but no written retention playbook exists. The persona-to-LTV linkage is not captured.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalize a 'brand continuity' checkpoint: after the Creator Identity step (see content finding above), ensure the OF bio, welcome message, and pinned PPV messaging reflect the same persona defined in social. Document this in the onboarding SOP (§6). For §9 fan retention, add persona-fit as a variable when analyzing whale and churn data.

consensus_risers.topic: Traffic continuity as model retention lever
consensus_risers.title: Treat consistent traffic delivery as the #1 model retention mechanism

variants.claim: Agencies that maintain traffic through IG downturns retain their creators; agencies that can't keep traffic flowing lose models and revenue simultaneously.
variants.what_they_do: This agency explicitly frames multi-channel traffic infrastructure (Meta ads + phone farm) not just as a growth tool but as a creator relationship tool. When IG accounts get restricted, creators see revenue drop and blame the agency. By having non-IG traffic running continuously, the agency absorbs IG volatility without it becoming a creator-facing crisis. The result: agency kept all models during a period when competitors lost creators, and received referrals from satisfied models.

variants.claim: Standardizing the entire delivery system so the creator is the only variable is what makes an agency scalable, reducing dependence on any single creator.
variants.what_they_do: Runs every avatar-fit creator through one standardized machine (onboarding, evergreen content verticals, welcome message, funnel, chat monetization) auto-triggered on signing; same delivery took one creator 12k to 282k/mo and another to 44k/mo with no extra custom work, at 1.6M/mo and 60% margins. Onboarding is 'complete' only after the first full week of delivery.

sources.quote: Models are leaving, your revenue is going down... we've been able to keep all of our models, even get referrals, whilst most agencies are losing their creators
you should be providing them traffic even when things are bad
Productized fulfillment just means the same delivery model for every creator and we're allowing the creator to be the variable
one will go on to make 280k, one will go on to make 44k... and the creator is the variable

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator retention strategy (§9 🔲 GAP) and no multi-channel traffic system (§5 🔲 GAP). If Palm's real creators are currently IG-dependent for traffic, they face the same churn risk described here. The connection between traffic reliability and model retention is not captured in Palm's current framework.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add 'traffic continuity during IG downtime' as an explicit SLA in creator agreements and as a tracked KPI in the whale-hunting / fan-tracker dashboards (§9). Build the narrative internally: Palm's job is to deliver traffic regardless of platform conditions. Use this framing in creator acquisition pitches (§6) as a differentiator vs. single-channel agencies.

consensus_risers.topic: Role onboarding with daily winning formula checklist
consensus_risers.title: Give every new hire a 'daily winning formula' — a repeating Notion task with linked SOPs for each duty

variants.claim: A repeating daily task assigned to each team role, listing every responsibility with embedded Loom SOPs or email templates, eliminates guesswork, prevents the employee from inventing their own process, and lets the founder delegate without micromanaging.
variants.what_they_do: For each role (editor, CSM, appointment setter, etc.): create a Notion task template called '[Role] Daily Winning Formula' that repeats every weekday. Each checklist item links to an SOP (Loom video transcript pasted into Notion) or has a toggle with an exact email template. Example CSM items: 'Maintain daily updates in Notion by 10am', 'Review and QC all video content within 4 hours of submission', 'Send content to clients for approval', 'Update client portal'. Task auto-populates each morning. Pair with a 30-day onboarding course (gallery of Loom recordings) and pre-day-1 through day-6 onboarding checklist.

variants.claim: Without an SOP for every role, a new hire requires weeks of founder handholding, negating the time savings of hiring and blocking agency growth.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis and his partner documented every job into SOPs before delegating. They reinvested 100% of revenue for the first 12 months into the next hire, next system, or software — taking no personal salary. The founder's job is specifically to build the SOP so the hire can self-onboard and replace the founder in that function.

sources.quote: daily winning formula is basically what we'll do with this... I make it a repeating task every weekday... this is a task that just populates every single day for the client success manager
the reason your team would suck is because you're not actually equipping them with things like this to actually do well
every time I found myself doing something repeatedly, I basically just documented it, systematized it using Notion, and then trained someone else to handle it
if you do not have an SOP for every single part of your business, every single job, every single role, then again, you're never going to make it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has Clerk roles (editor, ai_editor, chat_manager, creator) and an editor workflow with a day-counter task queue (§1, §10), but no documented daily winning formula, role-specific onboarding checklist, or SOP library linked to recurring tasks. The §10 gap notes no hiring criteria or scaling plan. This tactic is immediately applicable to Palm's editors and Amin (contractor posting role).
palm_comparison.recommendation: For Palm's two most active roles — editor and Amin (manual posting) — create a daily Airtable or Notion checklist with 5–8 items each, linked to existing SOPs (or quick Loom recordings). Specifically for the editor: items like 'check task queue, pull next 2 slots, upload to Cloudflare, submit for approval.' For Amin: 'check Telegram topic, post per schedule, reply /posted.' This is a low-effort, high-leverage move to reduce dependency on Josh/Evan for day-to-day direction.

consensus_risers.topic: Instagram DM outreach volume and rate-limit management
consensus_risers.title: Send 50 IG DMs/day; back off 5 below rate-limit trigger

variants.claim: Sending ~50 DMs per day from a personal IG account is the optimal beginner outreach volume, and you should drop 5 below whatever number triggers Instagram's rate-limit warning to avoid restrictions.
variants.what_they_do: Operator DMs from their personal IG account targeting creators with 5k–50k followers who mention or have OnlyFans. They monitor for Instagram's 'Check community guidelines / actions required' warning; whatever number triggered it, they permanently send 5 fewer. Follow-up sequence: wait 24 hours after no reply, then send a voice message for personalization.

variants.claim: Sending 60 outreach DMs per day (10/hour, 6 hours/day, 7 days/week) produces 1,680 messages per month, which is sufficient to sign at least one client.
variants.what_they_do: Agency instructs new operators to hit exactly 60 DMs per day split into 6 one-hour blocks. At that pace, 1,680 monthly contacts makes signing a single client near-certain. They also recommend tracking every outreach in a spreadsheet and following up with non-responders.

sources.quote: I want you to send 50 DMs per day
always send five below. Whenever you get that message
wait 24 hours and then send another follow-up from that point on. a voice message
For 30 days, if you do six 60 outreach messages a day, you can do 10 per hour, six times a day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented daily volume target, rate-limit protocol, or follow-up cadence (§6 GAP). No internal baseline on outreach message structure or follow-up timing exists.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a daily DM volume SOP: start at 50/day, instrument the rate-limit floor, and enforce a 24-hour → voice-message follow-up sequence. Document this in the acquisition runbook so it can be handed to a future acquisition VA.

consensus_risers.topic: Inbound creator acquisition via personal brand
consensus_risers.title: Build personal brand (YouTube + IG) so top creators DM you first

variants.claim: High-quality creators with good work ethic come inbound to agencies that have visible authority content (YouTube, IG reels), eliminating the need to chase them.
variants.what_they_do: Oliver posts OFM-focused reels on a dedicated IG account (~10K followers) and maintains a YouTube channel since 2023; creators DM him directly saying they're looking for an agency. He signs them on the first call. He explicitly states creators who DM you first already see you as an authority, while creators you have to chase tend to have poor work ethic.

variants.claim: The binding constraint on agency growth is obscurity, not lead supply, so building a known brand is the highest-leverage acquisition asset.
variants.what_they_do: Argues all growth is constrained by irrelevance; a brand compounds trust before the first conversation so a large share of pipeline becomes inbound, and reframes competition as obscurity rather than other agencies. Notes ~7M creators means the market is nowhere near saturated.

sources.quote: this girl called DM'd me on my OFM Oliver Instagram where I post a bunch of reels
the creators worth taking on…they usually come and find you. You don't try to find them.
your real constraint isn't leads. It's irrelevance. Nobody knows you exist
80% of my pipeline is from inbound, from my brand

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led outbound (§6 — THIN). No documented inbound content flywheel. This is the opposite motion: competitor earns inbound by investing in public-facing authority content, Palm currently spends effort chasing creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a public-facing OFM authority presence for Palm — even a small dedicated IG account posting agency results/tips reels. This converts acquisition from outbound grind to inbound pull, and pre-qualifies creators (they arrive already trusting Palm). Start with one reel/week; track how many creator DMs it generates over 90 days.

consensus_risers.title: DM prospects from a personal-looking account, not an agency page

variants.claim: Run creator outreach from a personal-looking profile rather than a branded agency account so messages get read.
variants.what_they_do: Send from your personal/duplicated account (lifestyle pics, normal-person vibe), never an 'OFM manager' agency account, because models skim their request folder and skip anything that reads as an agency. Stop labeling yourself 'OFM manager' anywhere; call it 'talent/influencer management' instead so you aren't lumped in with the spam.

variants.claim: Setting up a credible Instagram profile with professional photos (not casual/topless) is the primary vehicle for cold-outreaching creators, and showing your face builds trust that converts to signed deals.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner creates a personal IG account with polished location/lifestyle photos (real or Pinterest-sourced). Shows face for credibility; faceless is acceptable if IG photos are professional. DMs creators who appear stuck, plateaued, or post-bad-agency. Offers a free 1-week trial to prove value, then converts to 50/50. Moves promising leads to FaceTime to close.

sources.quote: most people send their messages out from like an agency account... I would actually highly advise you to do your personal account.
please stop saying OFM manager in either your username, your bio... try to be a little bit different... talent management agency.
you want to just make an Instagram profile, you want to set it up some nice business photos
you can offer if you find a model and you believe in yourself… I'll work for free for you for a week and if things improve then we can go to a 50/50 split

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm acquisition is Josh-DM-led + referrals with no documented outreach-profile standard (§6 THIN/GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Standardize an outreach profile spec (personal-style account, lifestyle content, non-'OFM' positioning) as part of Palm's acquisition playbook.

consensus_risers.title: Build a perpetual stockpile of karma-farmed Reddit accounts via VA team

variants.claim: Running Reddit at scale requires a dedicated VA farming team that continuously creates and seasons accounts to a minimum karma threshold so you always have ready inventory to rotate in when accounts get banned.
variants.what_they_do: Agency runs a team of VAs (plus automation) that create Reddit accounts daily, warm them for 2-3 days acting as normal users, then farm post karma (target: 2,000) and comment karma (target: 300) by posting in meme/advice subreddits. Accounts are stockpiled; when a posting account gets banned a seasoned replacement is pulled immediately from the pile. VAs are paid per account that hits the karma threshold. Automation now supplements VAs for scale.

variants.claim: Creating Reddit accounts from scratch and warming them organically produces longer-lasting accounts than buying, because device-fingerprint mismatches on purchased accounts trigger faster bans.
variants.what_they_do: Agency creates accounts fresh, runs a 3–5 day warmup (scroll, join subreddits, 1–2 upvotes/downvotes/comments per day), then begins posting. Bought accounts are acknowledged as faster but riskier. Each account gets a unique tracking/trial link, unique content (spoofed), and unique captions so no two accounts can be chain-banned via shared signals.

sources.quote: we have an account creation system every day. there's new accounts being created and there's just a farming team
An account is done seasoning if it hits 2,000 post karma and 300 common karma
we run the accounts on mobile… mobile Reddit is a lot safer and the accounts last a lot longer than they would on web
the link should be different every account… nothing should link a different account to another because that's how you can get chain banned

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented Reddit strategy at all (§5/§2 GAP — traffic/distribution not documented). This is pure build-from-zero territory. Palm's Apify scraping and Publer infra exists for IG/TikTok but nothing maps to Reddit account farming or karma seasoning.
palm_comparison.recommendation: If Palm adds Reddit to its traffic stack, do NOT start with black-hat scale. Begin with the expert's own minimum viable advice: one dedicated VA, one phone, one account per creator, organic posting to well-matched subreddits daily. Only invest in a farming pipeline after proving per-creator ROI. Document karma thresholds (2,000 post / 300 comment) as acceptance criteria for any farmed account.

consensus_risers.title: Use Reddit profile bio as the single OF conversion point; layer IG or Twitter as intermediate funnel where direct OF links are banned

variants.claim: 99% of NSFW subreddits prohibit OF links in posts but allow them in the Reddit profile bio, making profile visits the core conversion mechanic; where even bio links are banned, route traffic through Twitter or Instagram as an intermediate step.
variants.what_they_do: Standard setup: OF link in Reddit profile bio only (not in post body or comments). Post content drives profile clicks; profile bio converts to OF. Where subreddits ban OF bio links (e.g. after saturation): place IG or Twitter link in bio instead, grow those accounts as a secondary funnel, then convert to OF from there. This also unlocks access to additional subreddits that block OF links but permit social links. Reddit DMs used sparingly as a tertiary funnel (limited by Reddit ban risk on high-volume DM sending).

variants.claim: Outsourcing Reddit and X content distribution to a specialist contractor can yield disproportionate revenue relative to cost (cited: $1K/month spend generating ~$20K/month in revenue).
variants.what_they_do: Agency pays a Reddit/X specialist contractor roughly $1,000/month to distribute content across relevant subreddits and X accounts, funneling traffic to OF. The contractor is found through the agency's Telegram network. ROI is tracked as revenue attributed to that traffic source versus contractor cost.

sources.quote: basically 99% of the subreddits allows you to post it on your profile on your Reddit profile in the bio. That's it.
instead of getting all these fans and getting to getting them to the only fans straight away… you could also say, okay, I want to funnel them to, for example, an Instagram account
I pay him like a thousand bucks a month and he brings me like $20,000 a month
your Reddit and X contractors are distributing content and funneling into loads of subs turning into hefty profit

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no Reddit funnel documented (§5 GAP). Palm does run IG accounts and has Publer scheduling (§5), so the IG intermediate-funnel variant aligns with existing infrastructure. The Twitter/X layer is absent from Palm's current stack.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When testing Reddit, default to OF link in bio as the primary CTA. Before launching a creator on Reddit, audit which target subreddits permit OF bio links vs. only social links, and set up the appropriate intermediate account (IG preferred, as Palm already operates IG). Track Reddit → IG → OF conversion separately from direct Reddit → OF to measure intermediate-funnel efficiency.

consensus_risers.title: Main + 2-4 Burner Account Structure to Grow X Presence Fast

variants.claim: Running one premium 'main' account supported by 2–4 high-volume burner accounts is the core engine for rapid X follower growth and OF revenue.
variants.what_they_do: One main account is treated as the premium asset — high quality content only, no full nudity, goal of 50–100K impressions per post. Two to four burner accounts run parallel at higher volume (3–5 posts/day vs 1–3 on main), used for trend testing, engagement farming, and follower accumulation. Once burners hit 1–5K followers, all traffic is funneled to the main via pinned tweets, bio links, and reply CTAs. Main account holds the OF link; burners never monetize directly.

variants.claim: On X, a protected main brand account funneled by several disposable high-volume burner accounts maximizes growth while insulating the asset from bans.
variants.what_they_do: Runs one main account (2-3 quality posts/day, funnels to OF) plus 3-5 burner accounts (3-4 aggressive trend posts/day) that spam trends and pin replies funneling all traffic to the main; if a burner is banned, it's disposable.

variants.claim: X/Twitter is run as a deliberate dual funnel combining paid retweet-group 'drops' for reach and manual niche comment-baiting for targeted conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Funnel 1: pay a retweet-group manager to drop one image/day for 100-500 retweets; Funnel 2: build a comment bank plus 30-image SFW bank, follow ~1k niche accounts (warmed slowly, 5-10/hr), comment 50-100/day with short CTA captions under target posts; track via Fanvue tracking links targeting 3-5% click-to-sub CTR.

sources.quote: You're going to have two to four accounts in this depending on where you're at. The account one is going to be your main account.
These are your growth engine
I call it the twofunnel system
A good CTR is 3 to 5%

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's traffic/distribution strategy is a documented GAP (§5). Palm has no X/Twitter presence or strategy documented. This is a fully absent channel for Palm.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a 1-main + 2-burner X account structure for each real creator. Assign a VA to run burner engagement (spam commenting, trend monitoring, retweet loop) daily. Main account managed by editor/ops. Start with one creator as a test before rolling out.

consensus_risers.title: Bootstrap Threads from existing IG account for follower carry-over

variants.claim: Creating a Threads account from an existing Instagram account gives an immediate follower boost that accelerates early growth.
variants.what_they_do: TDM connects each creator's existing IG account to Threads so that existing Instagram followers automatically carry over at account creation, providing an instant audience base rather than starting from zero.

variants.claim: High early posting frequency on Threads accelerates follower growth because the platform still incentivises new content volume.
variants.what_they_do: TDM posts 5–8 times per day per Threads account in the first weeks. Once the account hits 1,000 followers (or after a couple of weeks), they reduce cadence to 3–5 posts per day. They mix carousel posts (highest performers) with shorter comment/text posts as filler.

variants.claim: Threads algorithmically amplifies brand-new accounts' first posts, making it faster to gain initial traction than Twitter/X, where momentum takes much longer to build.
variants.what_they_do: Create a new Threads account, post immediately — the first post gets organic reach from Threads' push. Content is simple engaging text (e.g. 'somebody FaceTime me' style posts targeting lonely men). Bio links to Telegram rather than directly to Fanvue, then Telegram DMs are used to warm and convert. Positioned as a 'hidden gem' due to platform newness.

sources.quote: use an account that you already have on Instagram to make the threads account because this will give you a boost, a kickstart from all the followers that you have coming over
5 to eight posts per day per account. Now, after a couple of weeks or when you reach 1,000 followers, you can back off a little bit to three, maybe five posts a day
Threads is really underrated. It can bring in a lot of traffic especially right now because it's more of a newer platform
when you make a brand new Threads account or get a Threads account right off the bat, your first post is going to get views

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented Threads strategy (§5 GAP — traffic/distribution not documented). Palm does run IG accounts for creators (§1) but there is no recorded practice of extending those to Threads.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm sets up IG accounts for real creators, simultaneously create a linked Threads account to capture the follower carry-over benefit at no extra cost. Add Threads to the Phase 3 distribution rollout (batch-5).

consensus_risers.title: 7-day IG warm-up: algorithm training, niche engagement, delayed link

variants.claim: New social accounts are warmed for about a week of human-like activity and niche engagement before any adult link is added, to build trust score and avoid early bans.
variants.what_they_do: Day-by-day: matching Gmail/name, scroll+like niche reels to train the algorithm, neutral (non-model) profile pic, follow ~15 niche creators/day, engage and like older US/UK 'simp' commenters under competitor posts to pull them over (never DM), then post first reel ~day 7 and only add the link.bio funnel 2-4 weeks in when followers justify the risk.

variants.claim: Bought aged IG accounts need only a 3–5 day warmup (engaging OF creator posts, scrolling) before going live — longer warmups recommended by others are unnecessary in practice.
variants.what_they_do: Purchase aged IG accounts to skip karma/history building. Warmup = 3–5 days of scrolling and engaging with OF creator content only. Then set up bio with link.me or Linktree, profile photo (face + slight chest), pin three posts showing key body parts relevant to niche. Post 2 Reels/day. For account isolation: UK/US/AU/CA creators can use home Wi-Fi unless ban rate exceeds ~10 accounts; otherwise use VPN/proxy.

sources.quote: how you set up and warm up an Instagram properly within 7 days to avoid being banned
Build up your Instagram account slowly and patiently
we buy our accounts aged and then we do a warm up for around like… 3 to 5 day warm up of just interacting OF creators engaging with the posts scrolling and that's it
IG is not as strict as people think it is… if you're in the UK, US, Australia, Canada… then you can use mobile Wi-Fi

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI warmup is mature and fully specified (§4 90-day cadence) but real-creator warmup is a documented gap (§4 THIN); this gives a concrete short-form warmup playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adapt a documented short warm-up + delayed-link checklist for new real-creator IG accounts (algorithm training, niche engagement, no-DM rule, risk-to-reward link timing) to fill the §4 real-creator gap.

consensus_risers.title: Every reel needs hook, retention, reward; 2-3/day across content pillars

variants.claim: Reels are the primary growth lever and each is engineered with a 3-second hook, mid retention and a payoff, produced at 2-3/day split across a few fixed content pillars.
variants.what_they_do: Treats reels as the top of the IG pecking order (reels > posts > stories > funnel); structures each as hook (first 3s) / retention / reward (e.g. an ugly-to-hot transformation); defines ~3 niche content pillars and split-tests them at 2-3 reels/day, doubling down on winners and cutting losers; moved from static text-on-image reels to 'clean motion' video.

variants.claim: Posting 2–3 Reels per day split between algorithm-maintenance lifestyle content and virality-engineered vertical content is the primary Instagram growth method for driving OF subscriptions.
variants.what_they_do: 2–3 Reels/day. Filler content = lifestyle/personal content to keep the account active and algorithm-fed. Vertical content = explicitly engineered for virality — Snapchat-style verticals, talking-head videos, mirror videos. Both types use 'link in bio' and pinned comments directing to OF. Competitor analysis is central: identify top-performing competitor videos, isolate the 3 best, test posting similar styles, then use performance data to teach the creator what to film and why it works (lighting, facial expression, movement).

sources.quote: Every reel should have a hook, a retention and a reward
a minimum of two quality reels per day
Filler content is just going to be her lifestyle. This keeps the algorithm happy… Vertical content is what brings new people into your page
Find three competitors. Make a list of the top performing videos. Take the three most performing videos. Put that in a style selection process

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's inspo pipeline scores viral reels and decides what to film (§1 MATURE) but does not codify per-reel hook/retention/reward structure or a pillar split-test cadence; real-creator cadence is a gap (§1).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a reel-structure standard (hook/retention/reward) and a pillar-based split-test posting cadence to the inspo pipeline output for real creators; complements Palm's existing scoring engine.

consensus_risers.topic: Creator brand building as a distinct discipline from niche selection
consensus_risers.title: Build distinct creator brands (audience + identity + story) — not just niches

variants.claim: Most OFM agencies confuse 'brand' with 'niche,' but a real creator brand answers three questions: who is your audience, what is the creator's unique identity, and what story/fantasy are you selling.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant uses a three-question brand framework — (1) specific audience demographic beyond 'men who like porn', (2) unique creator identity that inspires devotion, (3) specific fantasy/story and emotional levers to pull — and claims this framework has taken creators from zero to top 0.1% earning hundreds of thousands per month. He cites Camila and Bonnie Blue as examples of brand-driven success.

variants.claim: A distinct creator persona (identity, consistent theme, story) is replacing raw posting volume as the driver of growth, conversion, and ban-resistance.
variants.what_they_do: Defines a brand as persona + consistent theme + story (e.g. dominant/funny/confident); reports a branded creator going 2k to 50k/mo in two months, and writes a one-sentence persona applied to bios, captions, content, and chats.

variants.claim: Building a written character/persona for each creator and aligning all content to it raises LTV by enabling a parasocial relationship rather than generic thirst traps.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media sits each new model down to create an in-depth character sheet (occupation, personality, persona, backstory) and aligns outfits, scenes, and PPVs to it, deliberately targeting spending demographics by the model's look so chatters can monetize a fleshed-out character.

sources.quote: most of OFM doesn't know what brand actually means or confuses the concept of brand with niche
brand and only fans boils down to three questions. The first question is who is your audience? The second question is what is your identity? The third and final question is what is your story?
make it that her persona
you're actually a character with a story and depth... build a better parasocial relationship with them

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator brand framework (§2 GAP and §1 partial). The content pipeline (§1) is strong on viral ideation but does not appear to systematically define creator identity, target audience persona, or narrative/fantasy positioning before deciding what content to film. This is a meaningful strategic gap in how Palm directs creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a structured brand-definition step upstream of the content pipeline (§1). Before a creator enters the inspo/filming cycle, document: (1) their specific target audience segment, (2) their unique identity differentiators vs. comparable creators, (3) the core fantasy/emotional narrative they sell. Store this in the Creators table in Airtable and use it as a filter when promoting inspo reels to creators in Step 5 of the pipeline.

consensus_risers.topic: Organic social as primary agency scaling lever
consensus_risers.title: Scale organic social ops as the #1 agency growth lever in 2026

variants.claim: The largest OFM agencies earning hundreds of millions annually built their scale on excellent organic social content years ago, and the rest of the industry must catch up now.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant frames organic social content production — not chatting infrastructure or paid acquisition — as the defining competency separating top-tier agencies from the rest, calling for agencies to laser-focus on producing excellent creative and scaling their social media operations.

variants.claim: Boosting existing organic posts performs poorly as paid traffic; scripted video ads designed specifically for conversion (with hard CTAs) significantly outperform.
variants.what_they_do: Agency writes 5–10 distinct video ad scripts per model designed for paid promotion — not repurposed from organic content. Scripts follow a narrative/scenario structure (e.g. boyfriend-behind-camera POV, creator implied to be on OF). Each includes a hard CTA so viewers understand she has an OF page. Creator films all scripts as a batch. Agency tests all creatives at $40/day for 7 days, identifies winner(s), requests variation batch of the winner format, and continues iterating. Distinction from organic content pipeline: purpose, format, and CTA are all different.

sources.quote: 2026 is the year you have to get good at organic social. It is time to laser focus on producing excellent creative and scaling your social media ops
the platforms merely ask for one thing in return. Just stop making dog shit. Create content that people actually want to watch
I'm talking about actual creatives made specifically for this
we'll give five to 10 scripts for video ads per model... we'll run a testing phase for these ad creatives

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already has a mature, data-driven organic social pipeline (§1) built around viral inspo scraping and an 8-step production workflow. Palm is ahead of most competitors here. However, Palm's real-creator posting cadence (frequency, time-of-day) is not codified (§1 GAP), which limits the ability to truly 'scale' social ops systematically.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Palm should codify the real-creator posting cadence (frequency, platform-specific timing) to complete the §1 system. Additionally, document Palm's organic social capability as a formal competitive differentiator for use in creator acquisition pitches (§6). Consider producing case-study evidence of social growth results to use in signing conversations.

consensus_risers.topic: Reddit asset spoofing to prevent cross-account detection
consensus_risers.title: Spoof every reused image via @spoofsybot; use RedGIFs re-upload to spoof videos

variants.claim: Reusing un-spoofed images or videos across Reddit accounts links them and triggers chain bans; spoofing tools strip the metadata/hash that Reddit uses to connect accounts.
variants.what_they_do: For images: run all reused assets through @spoofsybot on Telegram (paid, cheap, offers free trial) before posting. For videos/GIFs: upload fresh to RedGIFs each time — RedGIFs auto-generates a new file fingerprint, functionally spoofing the video. Never repost raw files across accounts.

variants.claim: Platforms now algorithmically detect AI-generated images via invisible watermarks/metadata, so operators must avoid watermarking generators and look human to survive ban waves.
variants.what_they_do: Blames a wave of Instagram bans on Nano Banana Pro images carrying Google SynthID + metadata that Meta detects and flags as fraud/deception; fix is to generate with tools that leave no SynthID/metadata, avoid OF/Fanvue logos and links in bio, list multiple socials to look human, and use lower-profile link providers (Linktree over flagged Link.me).

sources.quote: spoofsybot on Telegram… you can spoof images there and you reuse content. Make sure you always do if you want to ever reuse content, you have to always spoof it
Red GFs technically does it for you because all you have to do in red gifs is just upload the video again and use a different link
Google Nano Banana Pro has, what we discussed earlier, something called a Synth ID
the most common ban reason is fraud and deception

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a mature AI-persona account isolation system (§3 — hardware-isolated, separate SIMs, GrapheneOS) but no documented asset-spoofing workflow for Reddit or any platform. This is a direct gap when scaling multi-account Reddit traffic.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add asset-spoofing as a mandatory step in any Reddit posting SOP: integrate @spoofsybot into the content-prep checklist for images and enforce RedGIFs-only video uploads. Document this in the platform-risk runbook alongside existing device-isolation procedures.

consensus_risers.title: AI personas remove model-dependency risk and keep 100% of revenue

variants.claim: Operators argue an owned synthetic creator beats managing real talent because it removes model-dependency, agency-hopping and split-revenue risk while keeping all earnings.
variants.what_they_do: Frames traditional OFM as a 'casino' (lazy models, theft, unpaid invoices, agency hoppers) where a $24k month nets only ~$6.5k after split; an AI model on Fanvue keeps 100% (minus 20% platform fee) for ~$17.5k net, fully owner-controlled and self-reliant.

variants.claim: AI creators can't use OnlyFans, so run them on an AI-friendly platform like Fanvue and prioritize realistic presentation over obvious perfection.
variants.what_they_do: OnlyFans does not allow AI; Fanvue welcomes AI, lets you label the account AI, and takes 15% vs OF's 20%. Build the persona like Build-A-Bear (face to niche, then body), batch content for both social and the paid platform, make the IG bio/handle look like a real person (uni, city, age), then funnel reels traffic to Fanvue and monetize via subscription + tips + PPV. Same can scale to ~$3-8k/mo on one model with no revenue split.

sources.quote: you get 100% share obviously with this business model
It's a casino because that's basically what it is
Only Fans does not allow AI models. It takes 20% fees... Fan View... they advocate AI creators. They take 15%.
The more realistic you make it, the better your results will be... bio that maybe says which university they're in... which city that they live in.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm runs real creators on OnlyFans today with revenue split (§0) and treats AI personas as a future scaling lever (§3 MATURE). Palm's AI personas still funnel to OnlyFans via social, unlike the AI-creator/Fanvue model taught here.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Hold for the future AI-creator line: validate the owner-keeps-100% Fanvue economics as the business case for a Palm AI line, separate from the real-creator OnlyFans core.

consensus_risers.title: Real models beat AI on connection, customs and platform access

variants.claim: AI personas underperform real creators because most OFM revenue comes from personal connection and custom content, and AI is pushed off OnlyFans onto less reliable platforms.
variants.what_they_do: Argues AI-model courses are 'selling shovels in a gold rush', that the real money is in fans believing they're talking to a real girl and in customs AI can't do, that OnlyFans won't host AI so you're forced to Fanvue (which pays out unreliably), and that referrals from happy real creators are the best growth engine.

variants.claim: Treat an AI creator's from-scratch follower build and 'too-perfect to be real' suspicion as core risks, and prefer scaling a real creator with existing momentum.
variants.what_they_do: Argues AI OFM is overhyped: content is limited (close-to-camera, props/Ferrari emblems look wrong, constant proof-checking, costs per generation), no real customs, no collabs/podcasts/in-person viral content, and you start from zero followers where a '10/10' new account looks suspicious. His own AI creator scaled to only ~$25k with huge effort; he prefers taking 50% of a real creator's fast growth over 100% of little.

sources.quote: they are all pretty much trying to sell you shovels in a gold rush
if you don't get paid, you don't get paid
you're going to be starting with an AI creator from scratch. like zero followers... how suspect it looks if your AI creator is like a 10 out of 10 chick.
we scaled a creator to $25,000. But the amount of time, energy, and effort that took. It is just not worth it compared to just working with real life creators.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Tempers Palm's AI-persona thesis (§0/§3): Palm's AI personas still funnel real social connection to OF, but this flags the customs/whale and platform-access limits and the Fanvue payout risk for any future AI-creator line.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Keep Palm's AI personas as a social/scaling lever while concentrating customs/whale monetization on real creators; treat Fanvue-class platforms and payout reliability as explicit risks for any AI-creator expansion.

consensus_risers.title: Set hard minimum PPV prices: $19.99 images, $39.99 videos, $300 customs

variants.claim: Selling PPV content below ~$20 for photos and ~$40 for videos trains fans to see the creator as cheap and destroys long-term monetization.
variants.what_they_do: This agency enforces hard price floors: no image PPV under $19.99, no video PPV under $39.99, no custom under $300. Low pricing is treated as a disqualifier, not a conversion tool. The logic is anchoring — cheap prices signal low value and attract fans who won't spend more, wasting chatter time for minimal return (framed as bad 'cash-for-effort' ratio).

variants.claim: Pricing PPV content by video length with a hard floor of 1 minute and a practical ceiling of ~$20 maximizes purchase rate across different fan spending levels.
variants.what_they_do: Short or clothed videos: under $8. Longer or more explicit videos: $8–$20. Hard rule: never charge for content under 1 minute — that goes free. Photos posted free to subscribers (subscription covers them); photos only charged if custom. Sends a mix of price points so lower-budget fans still convert on cheaper items while high-spenders can hit the $20–$25 ceiling.

sources.quote: you're now going to be selling pictures for no less than $19.99 each
Customs I'm not joking no less than $300

if I send out a shorter video or like a twerking video or I'm clothed then it's somewhere under like eight dollars and if it's a little bit more than that then it's somewhere over eight dollars always under I would say twenty dollars

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model or price floors (§7, explicit GAP). No internal baseline exists for what creators should charge for images, videos, or customs.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Establish Palm's standard PPV price floor as agency policy: ~$20 minimum for images, ~$40 minimum for videos, ~$300 for customs. Document this in the creator onboarding materials and share with the external chat team as a standing instruction. This is a zero-cost, immediate revenue lever.

consensus_risers.topic: Tiered commission escalation to reduce signing friction
consensus_risers.title: Start at 20-30% commission and escalate by milestone to earn the full split

variants.claim: New agencies without proven results should offer a tiered commission structure — low percentage at signing, escalating to 50% only after hitting defined revenue milestones — to reduce creator risk and incentivize agency performance.
variants.what_they_do: One speaker describes his first deal: 30% upfront, moving to 40% at $15k/month, then 50/50 above $30k. The logic is explicit: 'I didn't know anything about this game' and going in at 50% without proof is unjustifiable for the creator. The escalator also creates aligned incentives — the agency is motivated to grow the account because their own take increases. A complementary tactic: for established creators already making (e.g.) $5k/month, guarantee that floor no matter what the agency does in month one, demonstrating good faith.

variants.claim: Deal structure should flex to the creator's revenue tier instead of applying a single 50/50 split across all creators.
variants.what_they_do: Uses tiered economics: beginners via a paid coaching/productized model (not 50/50), mid-tier (10-50k/mo) on 50/50 plus an upfront setup fee, and whales (100k+/mo) on a lower rev-share plus a fixed monthly retainer (e.g. 20k/mo retainer + 50% of upside above current revenue) so the agency is profitable from month one.

sources.quote: my first Creator I went in at 30% and I said at 30% I will start if we get past 15K it'll move to 40 and if I can get you past 30k we'll go 50/50 from there moving forward
you have to have tiers... Your deal structure has to flex to the tier
I'm going to charge you 20K a month retainer... I only want 50% of what we make you above that 100K

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % at onboarding as a fixed field (§7), but there is no documented tiered/milestone-based commission model or floor-guarantee tactic for established creators. No internal baseline exists for how Palm's splits are structured or justified. This is a §7 gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Develop a documented commission framework with two tracks: (1) New creator / unproven account — start at 30-35%, escalate to 50% upon hitting defined monthly revenue threshold (suggest $10k/month, consistent with the 3-month target the speakers reference). (2) Established creator already earning — offer a monthly floor guarantee equal to their current earnings; charge full split only on revenue above that floor in month one. Codify both in the onboarding contract wizard.

consensus_risers.topic: Price integrity / repackaging instead of discounting
consensus_risers.title: Never fold on PPV price — repackage content instead of discounting

variants.claim: When a fan doesn't purchase at the stated PPV price, cutting the price cheapens the brand; instead, trim or repackage the same video at a lower price point so it appears to be different content.
variants.what_they_do: If a fan ignores or declines a PPV, the chatter does not reduce the listed price. Instead, they clip the same video to a different length, change whether a thumbnail is shown, or rebundle it, then re-pitch it as a 'different video you might like' at a lower price. This preserves the perceived value of the original piece.

variants.claim: Reflexively discounting when a subscriber says 'too expensive' trains them to always haggle; instead, offer more content at the same price first, and if a discount is unavoidable, remove content from the bundle rather than simply lowering the stated price.
variants.what_they_do: Rule 1 — Value-add first: if a sub resists price, add a small extra (bonus clip, picture set) and make it a huge deal, framing it as a personal favor. This gets full price paid and doesn't establish a discount expectation. Rule 2 — Renegotiation discount: if discounting is unavoidable, pre-load the PPV with a few extra pictures/clips by default. When the sub asks for less, remove those extras and drop the price — so the price reduction is tied to a concrete content reduction, not arbitrary. This preserves the perception that prices are meaningful. Rule 3 — Underpriced sub recovery: if a sub accepts a dropped price too readily, immediately propose a larger bundle ('I had an idea — I want to throw in extra stuff') to recapture upside.

sources.quote: if there's a point where they don't purchase don't fold on your pricing what you can do though is you can trim videos make them different lengths repackage it so it doesn't seem like you are folding
it is a better first response to try and give them something small to sweeten the deal, and you get paid the full amount as opposed to just discounting
if they ask for a discount, you can take that stuff out and then drop the price, so you've now renegotiated

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal PPV pricing model or price-integrity policy is documented at Palm (§7 GAP). This is a concrete tactic for the in-house chat and pricing buildout.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'no-discount, repackage only' rule to the future chat team SOP. Build a small asset library of clip variants (different lengths, thumbnail on/off) for each PPV set so chatters have ready-made repackaged versions to offer.

consensus_risers.title: Send blurred/cropped preview before full PPV to spark curiosity and drive unlock

variants.claim: Sending a blurred or cropped content preview instead of the full asset creates curiosity that converts fans to purchase the unlock.
variants.what_they_do: Rather than describing content verbally or sending it outright, chatters send a teaser (blurred image or cropped clip) accompanied by a curiosity-hook line such as 'guess what's under this honey, unlock it to find out.' The preview functions as a visual purchase prompt.

variants.claim: Sending free 'outfit' or soft-tease images before the first paid offer raises arousal and reduces friction on the first purchase, making the entire escalation ladder more likely to convert.
variants.what_they_do: At the start of each scripted session, the chatter sends 1–2 free images — typically a suggestive but non-explicit 'outfit' shot — with no price attached, framed as casual sharing ('what do you think about this outfit?'). Only after the subscriber reacts positively does the chatter pivot to the paid ladder. This gates the sell behind a micro-engagement that confirms interest and builds investment.

sources.quote: use teasers and previews instead of sending the full content immediately send a blurred or cropped preview to spark curiosity
I'll maybe send you a couple pictures just for free no price attached of my outfit maybe I look cute I'm like yeah what do you think about this outfit
ideally it's something sexual that makes the subscriber horny and he's like okay yeah that's hot... now after that you can be like yo do you want me to show a bit more

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no internal PPV sequencing SOP (§2 THIN, §7 GAP). This is a concrete mechanical step missing from any documented Palm chatting or PPV workflow.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the blur/crop preview as a standard step in the PPV send sequence for both the outsourced chat team (share as guidance) and future in-house SOP. Editors/content team should produce a 'teaser variant' of each PPV asset at upload time to make this operationally easy.

consensus_risers.title: Weekly async Loom chat reviews focused on new subscribers + high-ticket purchases

variants.claim: Regular async video reviews (via Loom) of specific conversation types — new subscribers, purchases, high-ticket sales — plus chatter-submitted chats drive measurable skill improvement.
variants.what_they_do: Manager opens the 'new subscribers' tab and reviews those conversations each week. Also reviews purchase logs and high-ticket sales. Asks each chatter to submit specific chats for review. Records Loom feedback videos. Per-review focus stays on one improvement point. Maintains 'scientist mode' (critical, constructive) not 'dictator mode' (anger-driven). Each chatter gets one weekly improvement point plus actionable steps.

variants.claim: Systematically monitoring which specific messages generate the most engagement and sales, then sharing winners as team templates, compounds chatter performance over time.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to set daily sales targets, track which message styles and scripts convert best, and collaborate with teammates to share high-performing templates. If a flirty line is working across multiple fans, it gets codified as a reusable template for the whole team.

sources.quote: We open up the new subscribers tab and read those conversations. We look at purchases. We look for high ticket sales. And we ask chatters every week to give us specific chats to review
Every week we analyze the one point of improvement for each chatter
if a certain flirty message is working well for multiple subscribers you can use it as a template for future chats

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal review cadence at Palm (§8, outsourced). Partially relevant to the AI chatting project as well. Build-toward.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design a weekly review ritual into the in-house chat SOP from day one: manager records a ≤5-min Loom per chatter, logs the single focus point and action steps in Airtable (add a 'Chatter Reviews' table under the chat team's workspace).

consensus_risers.topic: Emotional experience framing vs. transactional selling
consensus_risers.title: Sell the intimate experience, not the content — fans pay for emotion, not pornography

variants.claim: Subscribers don't spend money on OF because they can't find free explicit content; they spend because they're buying an emotional, intimate experience that free sites can't replicate.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are trained to frame every interaction as building genuine intimacy — shyness, excitement, escalating personal connection — rather than a content transaction. The chatter script is designed so the fan feels like the experience is happening *with* the creator, not being sold a product. The script's pacing (slow → fast) mirrors real intimacy. This psychological framing is positioned as the core reason scripted chats outperform both mass PPVs and one-off custom content.

variants.claim: The girlfriend experience (parasocial connection, scripted live-feeling sells, no PPV spam) drives the highest and most durable chatting revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Optimizes the OF wall to feel like 'Snapchatting,' uses 7-10 stage scripts with verification images, never initiates sexual content (lets the fan initiate), handles broke fans with ego-protecting lines, and forbids PPV spamming across the whole team.

sources.quote: only fans isn't about sexuality it's about emotions and experiences
what he really searches for are the emotions the real sexual experience not just the pure sexuality
the GF experience, the only thing that I try to run in OFM

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) with no internal script or framing doctrine. This 'experience over transaction' principle should anchor chatter training philosophy when Palm builds in-house. Also directly applicable to AI chatting — arguably more so, since AI chatters must compensate for lack of a real person.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Make 'sell the experience, not the content' the #1 principle in the chatter training SOP. Require chatters to spend a defined minimum number of exchanges on warmup/small talk before any PPV offer. For the AI chatting project, build emotional escalation cues into the conversation state machine — track where in the 'intimacy arc' each fan is before triggering a sell.

consensus_risers.title: Configure an auto-sent paid intro message ($15–25) to fire on every new subscriber

variants.claim: Setting up an automated PPV intro message that triggers the moment a fan subscribes generates passive revenue from every new sub with zero chatter effort.
variants.what_they_do: Inside OF settings, a welcome message is pre-loaded with a PPV attachment priced at $15–25. It fires automatically to every new subscriber. No chatter action required. This is the first monetization touchpoint before any live chat begins, and it also flags whether the new sub is a likely spender (purchase = qualified lead for the upsell ladder).

variants.claim: An automated message sent to every new subscriber that creates a conversational scenario and pitches a first video purchase converts cold new fans into paying customers before a live chatter has to engage.
variants.what_they_do: A pre-written automated script goes to every new sub immediately after they subscribe. It creates a light interactive scenario (not specified in detail), leads the fan through a short interaction, and closes with a $34.99 video offer. Once the fan buys, they are flagged as 'warmed up' and handed to regular chat flow at full price floors.

sources.quote: another way that you can quickly gauge how interested a fan is and your content is if they purchase your intro message this is a message that you set up to automatically send out once they hit subscribe
usually our welcome messages are like 15 to 25 bucks but then the first ppv we send is like 5 to 15
it will send to the sub it will create a little scenario it will get them to do something and then it will sell them a video for $34.99

palm_comparison.vs_us: No Palm-documented equivalent (§8 outsourced, §7 GAP on PPV setup). This is a zero-marginal-effort revenue lever that the external chat team can implement immediately for every Palm creator, and should be baked into the in-house chat onboarding checklist.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Immediately brief the external chat team to verify every Palm creator has an auto-send intro PPV configured at $15–25. Make this a checklist item in creator onboarding (§6 form wizard). Track intro-PPV purchase rate per creator as a leading indicator of subscriber quality.

consensus_risers.title: Five-tier chat priority queue: active sext session first, time-wasters last

variants.claim: Organizing the subscriber inbox into five explicit priority tiers ensures chatters always work highest-revenue conversations first and never waste time on non-spenders while whales wait.
variants.what_they_do: Priority order: (1) fans currently in a sext/purchase session, (2) fans being transitioned into a session (teasing phase), (3) new subscribers (unknown spend potential — test them), (4) known spenders/whales in relationship-building, (5) time-wasters — only addressed when inbox is empty. Agency adjusts exact ordering based on where their revenue concentrates.

variants.claim: When managing multiple accounts simultaneously, chatters should prioritize responses to high-spending or long-tenure subscribers over the general queue.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters explicitly rank their open conversations: whales and loyal long-term members get first responses. Low-spend or new subscribers are handled after, often with templated messages to maintain efficiency without abandoning them.

sources.quote: number one in my opinion for everybody should be the ones that are currently in a sexting session
The last ones should be time wasters because they're just wasting our time
prioritize high value chats focus on subscribers who frequently spend money or longtime members

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a whale-hunting module (§9) and Fan Tracker CRM but no documented real-time chat prioritization queue. The outsourced chat team may have their own system, but Palm has no visibility into it.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, implement this queue as a visible status layer in the Fan Tracker CRM. Consider surfacing active-session and new-subscriber flags in the whale-hunting dashboard (§9) so the chat manager can monitor live priority distribution across creators.

consensus_risers.title: Standardize all scripts and objection-handling so mid-conversation shift handoffs are seamless

variants.claim: Without fully standardized scripts across shifts, handoffs mid-selling-sequence cause subscribers to disengage, and the agency loses $1–2K per day specifically at shift-change moments.
variants.what_they_do: Every script, objection-handling sequence, and selling approach is written down and standardized across all shifts. The standard is specific enough that if Chatter A stops mid-sentence, Chatter B can pick up the exact conversation thread without the subscriber noticing a change. Revenue tracking consistently shows the lowest revenue window is at shift change — this is the explicit problem they are solving.

variants.claim: Per-creator chatting is staffed with three eight-hour chatters plus one reliever, with documented handoffs for seamless 24/7 coverage.
variants.what_they_do: Runs 3 chatters (six days) + 1 reliever (three days), all 8-hour shifts; Discord clock-in/out, end-of-shift reports, and at-next-chatter handoff messages so in-progress conversations transfer without gaps.

sources.quote: standardize the scripts, standardize the objection handling, the selling ways. So if one chatter stops texting mid-sentence, the other chatter can jump in flawlessly
it's always the lowest revenue right when the shift shifts
Three chatters and one reliever

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting (§8) and has no internal script or handoff SOP. This is a critical build-toward finding: the revenue leak at shift change is quantified ($1–2K/day at scale) and the fix is documentation, not hiring.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When designing the in-house chat system, treat shift-handoff continuity as a first-class requirement. Build a conversation-state handoff field (current PPV sequence step, last offer made, fan emotional state, open objections) into whatever CRM or chat tool is used. Standardize scripts before hiring chatters — not after.

consensus_risers.topic: Fan list segmentation for mass messages
consensus_risers.title: Segment subscriber list into 5+ tiers; send different mass messages per tier daily

variants.claim: Sending the same mass message to all subscribers burns money; segmenting by purchase behavior and sending tailored tone, CTA, and price to each segment dramatically increases conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Minimum one mass message per day (online or standard). Lists segmented into at minimum: new subscribers, OC (old/churned) list, active selling list (have purchased content), big spenders, no-spenders, time wasters (high engagement, zero purchase). Each segment receives different message tone, CTA, and price point. A 500-dollar spender gets a completely different pitch than a subscriber who joined 8 hours ago. Iteration loop applied to mass messages: if view rate is high but reply rate is low, convert statements to questions ('Would you rather see me in the bikini or out of it?') — reply rate doubles.

variants.claim: Using OF's native list feature to segment fans by content preference (feet, striptease, etc.) and renewal status enables personalized PPV sends that feel direct and increase conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Creates named lists inside OF (beyond defaults): VIP list for high-paying subscribers ($200/mo tier who get PPV free), preference-based lists (feet fans, striptease fans, etc.). When sending a PPV mass message, selects the relevant list as the target or excludes VIP list from paid messages. Sends preference-matched messages to niche lists with personalized copy to increase perceived intimacy.

sources.quote: We segment the lists. new subscriber list, OC list, active selling list…big spenders, no spenders, time wasters
someone who's already paid 500 bucks doesn't need the same pitch as someone who just joined 8 hours ago

you can also create lists for certain people like if you know there are certain people that really like feet pictures or there's certain group of people that really like striptease videos you can add them to that list and then whenever you do want to send out a certain type of message you can send it to everybody

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) with no documented internal segmentation strategy. Palm's Fan Tracker / whale-hunting system (§9) collects fan spend data, which is the raw material for this segmentation — but there's no documented bridge from that data to mass message targeting. This is an actionable gap even before building in-house chat.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As an immediate step: share Palm's whale-hunting and Fan Tracker data with the outsourced chat team and request they implement list segmentation (minimum: new, active buyer, big spender, no-spender). As a build-toward step for in-house: define the 5 segment definitions in Airtable against fan spend fields and automate segment tagging so chatters can filter in CreatorHero or equivalent. For the AI chatting roadmap, list segmentation is the primary personalization input — design the data model to support it from day one.

consensus_risers.title: Agency founder must chatter personally before hiring chatters

variants.claim: Before delegating DM monetization to hired chatters, the agency operator must handle messaging themselves to understand fan psychology and what drives spending.
variants.what_they_do: Requires the founder to personally reply to DMs in the early stage — not as a permanent task but as a learning phase. The stated goal is to internalize why fans message, why they send money, and what emotional triggers drive PPV conversions, before writing SOPs or hiring people to do it.

variants.claim: Agency owners should personally do DM chatting for two weeks to understand fan psychology and upsell mechanics before hiring chatters, and should prioritize South African hires over Southeast Asian for native English slang and cultural alignment.
variants.what_they_do: New agency operators personally handle all DMs for roughly two weeks to learn what works. They then hire chatters from South Africa (preferred) or South America, paying in local currency (favorable exchange rate). Three chatters per creator account rotate 8-hour shifts for 24/7 coverage. Chatters are trained by the operator who now understands the product firsthand.

variants.claim: Founders who chat themselves get short-term quality gains but sacrifice the bandwidth needed to get results, attract better creators, and scale.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis explicitly stops chatting himself and redirects that time into training chatters to surpass his own skill level. He frames chatter training as a higher-leverage use of founder time than doing the chatting, because it unblocks bandwidth for content direction, acquisition, and results that attract higher-tier creators.

sources.quote: You need to understand the psychology. Why is this dude, you know, typing a message this girl online? Why is he here? Why is he sending money? You need to understand these things.
step two, is to hire chatters... the vast majority of our team is from SA, from South Africa. Mainly because English is their first language
if you are chatting yourself, you're not going to have that bandwidth — the key there is to train your chatters to become better than you

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources all chatting to an external team (§8) and has no internal SOPs, scripts, or fan psychology documentation. This means Palm's leadership may lack the ground-level insight needed to evaluate chatter quality or build in-house scripts for the roadmap in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As Palm moves toward an in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap, §8), leadership should run a time-boxed hands-on chatting sprint (e.g., 2-4 weeks on 1-2 accounts) to build firsthand understanding of fan psychology before writing scripts or hiring. Document findings as the foundation of Palm's internal chatting SOP.

consensus_risers.title: Always wrap up a sexting session before logging off to prevent churn

variants.claim: Abruptly ending a chat session after a subscriber has spent money makes them feel used and dramatically reduces the chance they return or spend again.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are explicitly instructed to close every sexting/monetization session with a proper conversational wrap-up — warm sign-off, leaving the subscriber feeling positive and wanting to return. Logging off mid-session is a fireable-level error in the VA training.

variants.claim: Sending a warm, smiling 'thank you' video after a fan's spending session — when he is emotionally most vulnerable — builds the relationship bond that drives repeat monetization rather than churn.
variants.what_they_do: When a fan signals spending fatigue, the chatter stops pushing PPVs entirely. Instead, the model sends a pre-filmed 'gratification video': short clip of her smiling, saying she had a great time, expressing genuine-seeming warmth. The chatter then attempts to extend the chat slightly rather than letting the fan leave immediately. The logic: the fan is emotionally open at this moment; a positive close imprints a good memory that makes him return for the full PPV ladder again next session.

sources.quote: always wrap up the conversation sexting session before logging off this is to leave the subscriber feeling good and wanting to come back for more next time otherwise if you abruptly stop replying the subscriber has spent hundreds of dollars on content they're going to feel used

this is just a simple video of the model saying thank you and that she had a really good time to the camera being happy smiling. So you make the fan feel good. This is where they're most vulnerable to relationship building
you repeat this process until the fan can't spend anymore. When the fan can't spend anymore, you don't want to force any more sales because this will just ruin the whole relationship

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) with no internal SOP. This 'session closure' practice is a key retention mechanic that should be codified in Palm's future in-house chatter SOP and AI chatting prompt instructions.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add 'session closure protocol' to the chatter SOP: before ending any active conversation, send a warm closing message (e.g. affirmation, teaser for next time). Make this a scored QA checkpoint when reviewing chatter performance.

consensus_risers.title: Build a model profile doc with chatting style, persona facts, and content inventory for chatters

variants.claim: A documented model profile — covering chatting tone, physical stats, hobbies, and personality details — is essential so chatters can maintain consistent fan relationships without breaking immersion.
variants.what_they_do: For every creator they build a profile that includes: existing chatting style (especially for account transitions), height, age, bra size, feet size, astrological sign, hobbies, and other facts that come up naturally in DM conversations. Chatters reference this doc before and during conversations. Content is stored in a shared Dropbox/Google Drive the model can also upload to directly.

variants.claim: A structured onboarding folder with three components — model info sheet, platform logins, and pre-made PPV content scripts — prevents miscommunication and enables the chatting team to start immediately.
variants.what_they_do: On signing, the agency shares a Google Drive folder. The model info sheet captures personal details (favorite color, food, pet names, etc.) that chatters need to maintain consistent persona in DMs. Logins for all managed platforms are collected at once. Pre-made scripts (photo/video sequences already shot) are also uploaded so chatters can sell PPV from day one. Expectations are set in writing: communication only via WhatsApp group, Monday–Friday 9–5 local time.

sources.quote: you need to understand their chatting style if especially if they're running their existing of account and you need to transition their chatting style to your Chatters
little things like their height age bra size feet size astrological sign hobbies things that just naturally get brought up during these conversations
within our Google Drive folder, we have three things. the model information sheets... the login... and then last but not least is the scripts

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's external chat team operates without a documented Palm-side model profile SOP (§8 — outsourced, no internal SOP). Fan Tracker CRM (§9) collects fan data but there is no equivalent creator-side profile that briefs chatters on the model's persona and voice.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a 'Model Brief' Airtable record linked to each creator in the Creators table. Fields: chatting tone/style notes, physical stats, hobbies, boundaries, content categories available. Share with external chat team now; becomes the foundation for in-house chatter onboarding when that roadmap item ships (§8 build-toward).

consensus_risers.title: Per-source tracking links expose where revenue originates

variants.claim: Operators run the funnel as a tracked numbers game: per-platform tracking links reveal where subs and revenue actually come from and surface conversion bottlenecks.
variants.what_they_do: A separate Fanvue tracking link per social account measures clicks, subs, revenue and per-source CTR; a low CTR (under 1-2%) flags a broken bio/CTA, X funnel or Fanvue setup to fix; spend-per-sub is calculated per source to decide where to invest time and money.
