variants.claim: Using a dedicated tracking link for each traffic source (e.g., one link for Twitter) lets you measure exactly how much OF revenue each platform generates.
variants.what_they_do: Each traffic channel (X account, IG, promo page, etc.) gets its own tracking/inflow link. Griffin shows a single Twitter tracking link that generated $186K in OF revenue, proving channel-level attribution. This enables decisions like 'Twitter is responsible for ~40–50% of this creator's revenue growth' rather than guessing.

sources.quote: a direct tracking link created on fan view for X only
A good CTR is 3 to 5%. Less than 1 to 2% means your funnel's weak
This is a tracking link in inflow from one of our Twitter accounts on our creator using this strategy and we've generated $186,000 through this link.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm tracks invoicing/data (§7, §9) and has Fan Tracker, but cross-channel sub attribution and funnel-leak diagnostics are not documented; traffic measurement is a gap (§5).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement per-source tracking links plus a per-channel CTR / spend-per-sub view for real creators to attribute OF subs to their social source and diagnose funnel leaks.

consensus_risers.title: Replicate local folder structure 1-to-1 on Google Drive as business-continuity backup

variants.claim: If the agency's local machine were destroyed, the business should still be able to operate — so every folder and file that is critical to operations is mirrored on Google Drive in the exact same structure.
variants.what_they_do: The agency maintains a Google Drive that mirrors the local computer: a top-level 'Raw' folder per creator containing sub-folders for raw content, photo grid, invoices, promotional assets, OF stories, and the services agreement (contract). The Raw folder is shared directly with the model (right-click → share access → pink highlight convention so the manager knows it is externally shared) so models can self-upload content.

variants.claim: Running account and content operations off spreadsheet-style databases with front-end interfaces is the pragmatic backbone for managing accounts at scale.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media stores 10,000+ social accounts (proxy, 2FA, status, creation date) in Excel/Sheets as the database with front-end interfaces on top, updated automatically by bots, and explicitly says Airtable is just a prettier version of the same database approach.

sources.quote: if my laptop dropped or fell in a pool or caught on fire or lightning hit it or a car ran it over could the business still operate if the answer is no then I back everything up to the Google Drive
the Royal folder pink that means that this folder has been shared with the model
the only thing better than a spreadsheet might be a slightly prettier spreadsheet in something like Air Table

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm uses Airtable + Cloudflare media for asset storage (§1) and Dropbox for invoice delivery (§7). A formal business-continuity / disaster-recovery policy for media assets is not documented. The model self-upload workflow exists (creators upload to become Assets) but the specific shared-folder colour-coding convention and the 'can the business function without this laptop' checklist mindset are not codified.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a formal backup policy: define which data stores are business-critical (Airtable, Cloudflare media, contract PDFs, invoices) and ensure at least one redundant copy exists outside the primary system. For creator onboarding, consider a shared Google Drive or Cloudflare folder (colour/tag-coded as 'creator-accessible') as the upload landing zone, mirroring the model-upload convenience described here.

consensus_risers.title: Generate a formal invoice every time a model payment is made to maintain a legal paper trail

variants.claim: Sending a professional invoice with every model payment creates a paper trail that protects the agency legally and signals operational legitimacy to creators.
variants.what_they_do: Every creator payment triggers an invoice document stored in its own 'Invoices' sub-folder inside the creator's Google Drive directory. The services agreement (contract) is also stored in the same creator folder for easy retrieval.

variants.claim: Consistent invoicing builds model trust because it creates transparency and accountability, making it irrational for the model to leave a reliable agency over a single payment.
variants.what_they_do: They invoice every creator weekly and explain the invoicing schedule during the onboarding call. They frame invoicing not just as revenue collection but as a trust-building tool — a paper trail that shows the agency is accountable. They also argue that a model generating consistent income has no incentive to leave, reducing churn.

variants.claim: Agencies worried about non-payment should invoice weekly rather than monthly, reducing the maximum amount at risk at any given time.
variants.what_they_do: Bjorn mentions weekly invoicing as a practical risk-mitigation option for agencies that are anxious about creators running with earnings. He frames it as a simple operational adjustment rather than a trust-control mechanism, keeping the relationship dynamic healthy while capping downside.

sources.quote: inside will be categorized like this...you'll also have a photo grid invoices because each time we send a payment to the model we create an invoice remember you've got to be professional you've got to leave a paper truck
always invoice your clients it's a way to build trust but they're also not going to run if you provide a quality service
if you like the source go join the Discord — I'm going to invoice them weekly

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already generates weekly invoices automatically via Puppeteer PDF + Dropbox + Resend email (§7), so the core behaviour is matched. Palm also stores contracts at onboarding (§6). The incremental difference is that this agency co-locates invoices and contracts inside the creator's shared Google Drive folder, making them accessible to the creator directly — Palm currently delivers invoices by email rather than a shared folder.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Minor enhancement only: consider adding a 'Documents' section to the creator's shared folder (or a creator portal page) where they can view all past invoices and their contract on demand, reducing support requests and reinforcing professionalism.

consensus_risers.title: Automate fan re-engagement so chatters focus only on inbound

variants.claim: Revenue scales when automated, rotating 'smart message' sequences trigger on fan activity to remove human error and free chatters to work only inbound conversations.
variants.what_they_do: Sets up Infloww smart-message 'nudge' collections that fire when a fan comes online (after a delay), rotates ~10 collections every two weeks so messages feel fresh, auto-unsends ignored sequences, and stops the sequence once the fan replies—so chatters never do manual outreach.

variants.claim: Standardizing creator output into a fixed weekly content format lets an agency scale production across many models with minimal model and founder time.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media (faceless francis interview) requires two 'sets' per model per week, each composed of part A casual SFW realism content, part B sexy social content, and part C a sexting set or PPV, so every model drops into the same box that feeds both the marketing and the sales machines on four-to-six hours of model time.

sources.quote: you want to completely remove human error from chatters
this feature has made me hundreds of thousands of dollars for all my models
every week the models have to deliver two sets. A set consists of A, B, and C

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's tech spine has Publer scheduling but no fan-CRM-driven re-engagement automation tied to chatting (§8/§9 PARTIAL).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Plan a smart-message-style automated re-engagement layer (activity-triggered, rotating, auto-unsend) for Palm's future fan CRM / AI-chat stack.

consensus_risers.title: Set explicit month-1/2/3 milestones with creator at onboarding

variants.claim: Managing a creator's expectations up front — covering communication channels, response SLAs, and realistic revenue ramps per month — is the single most important onboarding step.
variants.what_they_do: Agency defines and communicates: which platform to message on, guaranteed response windows, and what revenue/subscriber outcomes are realistic in months 1, 2, and 3. They crowdsource realistic benchmarks from a community of 1,000+ operators to give creators honest projections rather than over-promising.

variants.claim: A concrete, numeric performance guarantee (specific result in a specific window or you work for free) reduces perceived risk and differentiates from scam-wary creators' defaults.
variants.what_they_do: Uses guarantees like '2,000 paying subs in 30 days or we work for free' or 'clear buying activity in chats within 14 days or we fund your next shoot up to 1,000 dollars'; pairs the offer with one main angle (speed/risk/ease).

sources.quote: managing expectations so when I say managing expectations that includes when can she message you on what platform what can she expect in the first week in the first month the second month and the third month
if you don't get at least 2,000 paying subs in 30 days, we work for free until you do
choose one main angle... speed, risk, and ease

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented expectations-setting SOP or milestone framework at creator onboarding (§6, §10). There is no written-down month-by-month ramp guide. This is a gap — creator churn risk is highest in the first 90 days when results lag expectations.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a one-page 'Month 1 / Month 2 / Month 3' benchmark doc (subscriber count, revenue ranges, content volume) derived from Palm's existing creator data. Deliver it at contract signing and reference it in a kickoff call. Log agreed expectations in the Creators table so Josh can track against actuals.

consensus_risers.title: Commission-plus-bonus comp structure with daily ranked leaderboard drives consistent chatter output

variants.claim: A commission-only base combined with milestone bonuses, daily ranked leaderboards shared with the team, and regular chat reviews sustains chatter performance over time and is the structural reason revenue grew from ~$20k/month to $80k+.
variants.what_they_do: Daily: an assistant compiles each chatter's individual revenue and ranks them on a leaderboard shared with the whole team — publicly feeding ego and competitive instinct. Comp structure: commission only plus bonuses tied to achievable milestones (the presenter spent three days designing this mathematically; result was a ~25% revenue lift). Chat reviews: manager logs into accounts periodically, reviews a sample of conversations, records a screen-capture with verbal feedback, sends to the chatter. Every chat review correlates with a numbers increase. Pre-training filter used before formal training to screen candidates — only those who pass pre-training get to the actual training stage. Hiring criteria codified into a checklist (not all personality types are suited to the role).

variants.claim: Every team member should be financially incentivized by the growth of the specific business metric their role controls, so their personal interest and the agency's interest are identical.
variants.what_they_do: TDM structures compensation by role-specific output: media team paid on video performance, marketing team on traffic, chat team on sales closed, recruiting team on hires made. Each person wins when the business wins, but through the metric they personally control — not generic salary.

sources.quote: every single day I have my assistant compile a report of the rankings of all of the salespeople because I give out bonuses
my compensation and bonus structure which I believe is key to the continued motivation of my chatters… numbers have gone up about 25% as a result
media team are compensated for these videos doing well. Marketing team are compensated for more traffic. Chat team are compensated for more sales.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented chatter hiring criteria, comp structure, or performance management cadence (§8 outsourced, §10 THIN). This is the operational skeleton Palm will need when building the in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When scoping the in-house chat team build (~1yr roadmap per §8): (1) Design a commission + milestone-bonus comp structure before the first hire — do not default to hourly. (2) Implement a daily revenue leaderboard visible to the whole chat team. (3) Build a pre-training filter (written test + role-play) that candidates must pass before formal onboarding. (4) Schedule weekly chat reviews for each chatter, not just when numbers drop. These four structural elements can be planned now and should be in the in-house team design spec.

consensus_risers.title: Hire cheap offshore chatters but train them intensively and systemize the work

variants.claim: The cost-effective chatting model is hiring low-cost offshore chatters and de-risking them through heavy recurring training plus fully systemized scripts so output doesn't depend on their prior experience.
variants.what_they_do: Hires Filipino chatters at ~$2-3/hour via OnlineJobs.ph, distrusts 'experienced' chatters who spam PPVs, and trains them personally 4-5 times a week (~20-30 min) on his own systems so every chatter sounds identical and 'can't mess up'.

variants.claim: The correct chatter hiring filter is competence, reliability, and honesty — not existing chatting skill — because a motivated person can be trained to top-level in 2–3 weeks.
variants.what_they_do: Explicitly de-emphasize prior chatting experience as a hiring criterion. Instead prioritize candidates who demonstrate effort, follow-through, and personal motivation throughout the screening pipeline. Agency trains chatters to top level within 2–3 weeks once the right person is selected. The entire multi-stage funnel (attention checks, situational tests, live calls, Loom compliance) is designed to surface coachable, reliable people — not to find pre-trained chatters.

sources.quote: Go to online jobs.ph. Get yourself a Filipino chatter. Two $3 per hour
teaching your chatters at least three times per week for 30 minutes a day minimum
It's about you hiring competent, reliable, and honest people that you can teach the skill of chatting. Because we taught chatters in like two or three weeks to get to a top level.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no in-house chatters yet (§8) and no staffing/training model documented (§10 THIN); this informs the future in-house chat team's hiring and training design.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Plan Palm's in-house chat team around systemized scripts + intensive recurring training rather than relying on hires' prior experience.

consensus_risers.topic: Minimum viable team structure for one creator
consensus_risers.title: Three core hires outside chatting: account manager, SMM, and model-facing manager

variants.claim: An agency running even one creator needs three non-chatter roles: an account manager (OF vault/feed), a social media manager (IG/Reddit/Twitter), and a model-facing manager who relays communication between backend and creator.
variants.what_they_do: Account manager owns the OF side; social media manager owns all platform posting and content direction; a 'model manager' (ideally first-world, trusted) sits between both and the creator — giving her content lists, flagging trending sounds, celebrating milestones, and fielding her daily questions. This person acts as the Swiss-army-knife ops lead who reports to the founder daily on whether Reddit posts went out, tweets sent, IG up, etc. Founder only sets direction, not execution.

variants.claim: Free the owner by splitting the agency into core departments, each run by a manager the owner reports through, instead of doing everything personally.
variants.what_they_do: Typically three departments — client acquisition, marketing, chatting — each with a manager (e.g. chatter manager, marketing director, acquisition manager). The owner checks in with managers (not every IC) once each morning; managers run the ICs (outreach VAs, editors, chatters). Beginners can start with 3-6 chatters + one chatter manager. Trying to do it all solo makes scaling to $1M/mo 'physically impossible.'

sources.quote: account manager to just oversee the only fans account then you would have a social media manager to oversee everything that comes between all socials whether it be Instagram Tik Tok Reddit Twitter
you want to have three four main departments... Client acquisition, marketing, and chatting... have a manager in client acquisition, a manager in marketing, and a manager in chatting.
once in the morning, you check in with all of the managers.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's org (§10) is thin: Josh runs acquisition/strategy, Evan runs tech/ops, Amin handles manual posting, editors handle video, and an external chat team covers DMs. There is no documented model-facing manager role, no SMM-layer employee, and no internal account manager layer. Communication between backend ops and creators runs through Josh/Evan directly, which doesn't scale. Palm's SMM consolidation (§11) is a tech fix; this is the human-layer equivalent that Palm lacks.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define and hire (or designate internally) the three-role stack as Palm scales past 3-4 creators: (1) OF account manager per cluster of creators, (2) SMM coordinator owning platform execution, (3) model-facing manager as creator relationship layer. The model manager role in particular should be a priority hire before the next creator signing — it directly reduces founder bottleneck and improves creator retention (§9 gap).

consensus_risers.title: Source chatters from onlinejobs.ph, Donut Jobs, OFM Jobs — never Telegram

variants.claim: The three reliable hiring platforms for chatters are onlinejobs.ph, Donut Jobs, and OFM Jobs; Telegram chatters are consistently low quality.
variants.what_they_do: Post job listings on onlinejobs.ph, Donut Jobs, and OFM Jobs. Explicitly avoid sourcing from Telegram groups. Also mentions some Facebook groups and other Filipino hiring sites as secondary options.

variants.claim: Operators master each posting channel personally before delegating it to low-cost VAs with strict SOPs, reserving founder time for high-ROI work.
variants.what_they_do: Run IG/TikTok yourself for ~1 month to learn it, then hand off to $1-3/hr VAs with documented SOPs and image/comment banks; keep TikTok geo-matched (US/UK/AU poster, not India/Africa) because TikTok pushes by poster location; X and Threads are easily outsourced, but the high-ROI anchor (IG/TikTok) is mastered first.

sources.quote: post a job post on onlinejobs.ph, Donut Jobs, and OFM Jobs. Those are like the three main hiring sites. Don't get them off of Telegram.
easily outsource this to a VA for anywhere from a dollar to $3 an hour
master this better than anyone else can

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced to an external team (§8); no internal hiring process exists yet. When Palm builds its in-house chat team (~1yr out), it has no documented sourcing channels. This is a direct gap to fill.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm begins building its in-house chat team, establish onlinejobs.ph as the primary sourcing channel. Create a blacklist policy against Telegram-sourced chatters. Document this as the first step in the future chatter hiring SOP.

consensus_risers.title: Daily feedback cadence to accelerate employee ramp 7x vs weekly

variants.claim: Giving employees feedback every single day instead of once a week makes them progress seven times faster, cutting ramp time from seven months to thirty days.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner or a designated lead delivers individual feedback to every employee daily — not a team standup, but specific corrective or reinforcing input tied to that person's KPIs and tasks. This is paired with formal performance reviews on a recurring schedule.

variants.claim: Founders should delegate menial, repeatable work (posting, editing, thumbnails) early to keep the creative/strategic brain free for high-leverage output.
variants.what_they_do: Delegates short-form editing, posting, and other menial tasks while keeping idea-capture and filming in-house; recommends building a light team (editors, thumbnail/caption help) early to avoid burnout from doing it all.

sources.quote: give your employees feedback once a week, I give them feedback every single day. That means my employees are going to progress seven times faster
you want to start delegating your editing, your anything that's like menial like posting
burning out from doing it all yourself. That's a very other common pitfall

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented feedback cadence for editors, Amin, or any contractor (§10 — THIN). There are no performance review cycles or KPI dashboards per employee in the codebase. The closest analogue is the editor task queue in the portal, which surfaces output but not a feedback loop.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Establish a lightweight daily async check-in for each active role (editor, posting contractor, eventually chatters): a shared Telegram thread or Airtable comment thread where the reviewer flags one specific 'fix this' and one 'good job' per day per person. Formalise this before hiring the in-house chat team (~1 yr roadmap item, §8).

consensus_risers.title: Target creators who are plateaued, overwhelmed, or burned by a bad agency

variants.claim: The highest-converting creator prospects are those who are stuck at a plateau, overwhelmed by workload, or have had a bad agency experience — not cold beginners.
variants.what_they_do: Agency explicitly looks for creators who: (a) plateaued after initial growth, (b) are overwhelmed managing content + chatting alone, (c) had a bad prior agency and are open to a better partner. Uses these pain points as the outreach hook.

variants.claim: Creator acquisition works by finding help-seeking creators in social comment sections, outreaching across every platform with a curiosity-driven message, and pushing for a quick call rather than closing over DMs.
variants.what_they_do: Searches TikTok for OnlyFans-related videos and mines the comment sections for girls asking for help (a signal they lack an agency), outreaches on every platform she's on with a short curiosity message ('100 fresh paying subs every single day'), and immediately pushes to a 5-minute call.

sources.quote: you're looking for models that are a bit overwhelmed, they're a bit stuck, their accounts are being plateaued, maybe they've had a bad agency before
within the comment section will be girls doing Only Fans asking for help
you basically just want to push for a call straight away

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented ICP (ideal creator profile) or qualification criteria for outbound (§6). Josh-led DM outreach is ad hoc. Targeting burned/plateaued creators is a concrete, testable filter Palm could apply immediately.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define Palm's creator ICP in writing: earning $1K–$10K/mo, plateaued 60+ days, active IG but not growing, or recently left an agency. Use these as DM targeting filters on IG/TikTok. Add a qualification question to the onboarding form: 'Have you worked with an agency before? What happened?'

consensus_risers.topic: Reddit content strategy vs. Instagram content strategy
consensus_risers.title: Use high-volume 7/10 static images on Reddit — never repurpose IG hook-driven reels

variants.claim: Reddit rewards posting volume of decent static/short-clip content across many subreddits, not the single viral hook-driven reel optimised for Instagram; ten 7/10 images outperform two 10/10 videos on Reddit.
variants.what_they_do: Agency repurposes a bank of ~100 photos per creator to sustain a full year of Reddit posting without new shoots. Videos used on Reddit are simple (10-15 sec, body-focused, no hook/narrative) and the same clip is reposted across 100 subreddits simultaneously. No time-of-day optimisation — just maximum post frequency spread across subreddits. Goal is aggregate impressions across subreddits, not a single viral post.

variants.claim: Content quality is the single most important Reddit variable — good content can overcome mediocre subreddits, but bad content fails even in perfect subreddits.
variants.what_they_do: Start with a niche (e.g. feet), find subreddits by searching and crawling competitor model pages. Post in each; if a subreddit doesn't reach ~20+ upvotes, swap it for a new one. Simultaneously test content by finding top-performing posts within target subreddits and asking the creator to recreate them with a personal twist. Scale to 20 subreddits/account then plateau for a month before expanding further.

sources.quote: give me a hundred pictures like like have a creator give me 100 pictures and I can promote her for the rest of the year with those 100 pictures
I would much rather have 10 pieces of content that are like seven out of 10, then I would have three pieces of content, two or three pieces of content that are 10 out of 10
we basically test the subreddit… if it doesn't get like 20 plus uploads a day then we just change them out for new ones

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content system (§1) is engineered entirely around IG hook-driven reels — viral scoring, transcript analysis, inspo-driven filming. That pipeline produces the wrong content type for Reddit. Palm also has thin OF vault content (§2); Reddit uses a middle-ground asset type (SFW-ish or soft NSFW stills/clips) that neither pipeline currently covers.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a third content tier for Reddit: simple, high-quality still photos and 10-15 sec body-focused clips. These can be captured as a small add-on during existing shoots. Store in Airtable as a separate asset type ('Reddit Assets'). Do not route IG reels to Reddit — they are wrong-format and waste the hook work.

consensus_risers.title: Scrape a 100k-subreddit master list keyed to creator physical attributes, then niche-match posts

variants.claim: Rather than manually searching for relevant subreddits, build a scraped database of 100,000+ subreddits tagged by niche attributes so any new creator can be immediately mapped to every viable subreddit based on her physical characteristics.
variants.what_they_do: Agency built (or uses) a scraper that pulls every subreddit and tags it by niche (ethnicity, body type, fetish, etc.). When a creator is onboarded, her attributes (blonde, big breasts, e-girl, etc.) are matched against the database to generate her posting list. Volume over precision: one piece of content gets posted across every marginally relevant subreddit. Key insight: NSFW content also gets cross-posted into SFW subreddits (makeup, car repair, GeoGuessr) using curiosity/cleavage tactics to reach users who have never enabled NSFW settings.

variants.claim: Beginners should copy the structure and tone of high-performing captions in their target subreddits (not verbatim) and only develop original voice after accumulating posting experience.
variants.what_they_do: Find the most-upvoted posts in the subreddits being targeted. Extract caption patterns (length, phrasing style, call-to-action type). Write similar-but-not-identical captions. After sufficient volume, the operator develops intuition for original captions. Treat caption testing as iterative — same test-and-replace logic applied to subreddits and content.

sources.quote: what we would do is we would just have a scraper that would basically scrape every single subreddit that would or could be tied to either one of these niches. You'd build up a list of subreddits. You'd have a 100,000 subreddits

there's also a lot of say for work subreddits where people would just, you know, I don't know, ask for advice for piercings in their ears or or makeup advice. And you could also post in these subreddits
we take inspiration a lot of the time from bigger models that are doing well in the subre we post in… make it similar but not the exact same

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no Reddit presence (§5 GAP). Palm's content pipeline (§1) does use Apify scraping for IG inspiration, so the scraping capability exists — it would need to be redirected to Reddit subreddit discovery. The SFW infiltration angle is completely absent from any Palm workflow.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Commission a one-time scrape of Reddit subreddits tagged by adult-content niche to build a master lookup table. Store in Airtable alongside creator attribute fields. On creator onboarding, auto-generate a subreddit posting list. Prioritise NSFW subreddits first; layer in SFW curiosity subreddits (lifestyle, hobby) only once the core strategy is proven.

consensus_risers.title: Threads 30-slave-account follow-bot: 1,200 targeted leads/day via viral-post follower targeting

variants.claim: Running 30+ Threads slave accounts that each follow 40–50 recent likers of viral similar posts generates ~1,200 targeted daily leads who see the main account funnel.
variants.what_they_do: One main Threads account posts daily content with Fanvue link. 30–50 slave accounts each have bio text 'I'm more active here' tagging the main. A custom Chrome extension bot finds recently viral posts in the niche, filters likes by recency, and auto-follows those users (40–50 per slave per session). Notified users see a profile → visit main → subscribe. AdsPower browser manages all slave accounts. The bot is distributed via private Discord.

variants.claim: A single creator can be scaled across up to 15–20 Threads accounts with near-linear compounding returns if content and operational capacity allow.
variants.what_they_do: TDM runs multiple Threads accounts per creator (up to 15–20), treating content volume and staff/hardware as the only real constraints. They use a phone farm for IG as the mechanism for managing Threads accounts and explicitly avoid automation tools (schedulers/outlers) due to Meta's sensitivity, preferring manual or native posting.

sources.quote: You need 30 50 of these. They just have to post and in the bar you can say I'm more active here and tag your main account
30 slave accounts, multiply by 40 follows, 1,200 targeted leads a day, you will get subscribers

if you've got enough content and the content performs and you see return on investment, you could run up to 15 20 accounts per creator. The bottleneck here is often times the manpower to actually run that and the hardware

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented Threads strategy or follower-growth automation (§5 🔲 GAP). Palm does use Publer for scheduling but not engagement automation. This level of account farming on Threads is outside Palm's current documented ops.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the AI persona scaling line (§3), evaluate the Threads slave-account follow strategy as a traffic lever. The math (1,200 leads/day) is compelling if sustainable. Before adopting: assess Threads TOS risk (similar to IG follow-loop risks) and whether AdsPower fits alongside existing GrapheneOS/hardware isolation setup (§4). Start with a small 5-account pilot before scaling to 30+.

consensus_risers.title: Snapchat + read-only Telegram channel as a ban-resilient backup funnel

variants.claim: Operators add low-effort Snapchat reach and an owned Telegram broadcast channel to nurture leads and survive social bans, since owned audiences persist when accounts die.
variants.what_they_do: ~15 min/day Snapchat: public profile, repurposed 9:16 content, 2-3 stories/day, Telegram CTA only once/week at peak time, no DMs/links to avoid bans; funnels into a read-only Telegram broadcast channel (pinned Fanvue link, niche real-life teasing, reaction-bait) that both converts and acts as a 'safe haven' to relaunch socials after a ban.

variants.claim: Snapchat is an under-exploited OFM channel that simultaneously drives OF subs, cross-promotes other platforms, and pays direct creator monetization.
variants.what_they_do: Sets up a real-looking account, posts ~5 spotlights/day plus 30-100 clickbait story snaps/day (repurposed via repurpose.io or a VA), CTAs only once a week to avoid disabling, and uses paid promoted-snap boosts; cites a creator earning as much from Snapchat as from Instagram.

sources.quote: Over here we have the Telegram funnel
builds a safe haven for leads on your Telegram if a social ban occurs
boost your initial following

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's distribution is Publer + Telegram-to-poster (§5) with no documented owned-audience/backup-channel or Snapchat strategy; traffic strategy is a gap (§5).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Pilot an owned Telegram (or email) audience as a ban-resilient backup channel for real creators, plus Snapchat as a cheap secondary funnel; reduces single-platform dependency risk.

consensus_risers.title: Twitter/X is currently the strongest traffic platform; Reddit is high-potential but underutilized

variants.claim: Among organic traffic platforms, Twitter/X currently delivers the best measurable results for OF creator traffic, with Reddit expected to outperform once properly activated.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's agency client reports running Twitter (short teaser clips, slightly explicit), Reddit (entering subreddits, not yet fully active), Instagram (teaser photos + Reels), and TikTok-to-recreate. Twitter is confirmed as currently performing best. Dylan validates that Reddit, once properly worked, will be the strongest traffic source. He explicitly calls out that copy-paste trending TikTok tactics from Telegram groups are 'super outdated' — TikTok is no longer the viral free-for-all it was in 2022.

variants.claim: Twitter allows near-explicit content, making it a materially higher-virality channel for OF funnels than IG/TikTok — agencies should set it up at onboarding and teach creators to reply to viral tweets.
variants.what_they_do: Agency sets up Twitter for any creator who lacks it during onboarding, teaches creator to post more explicit (but compliant) content and to reply to trending/viral tweets to capture organic reach. Frames Twitter as a separate traffic channel from IG/TikTok, not a duplicate.

sources.quote: Twitter's the best yeah Twitter okay so Twitter's the best okay um Reddit though would weigh when we start getting that going so I know that's very good for traffic
that's like I would say that's like in my opinion like a super outdated method like it's very like TikTok is completely different people don't understand
on Twitter you can get away with more not safeer workor content so AKA showing everything and the chance of that going viral is a lot higher and Twitter's full of that right

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented traffic strategy across platforms (§5 GAP). The tech spine routes content via Publer and Telegram but there's no prioritization of which platforms drive OF conversions or how to activate Reddit. This finding gives a concrete platform hierarchy: Twitter first (activate and optimize), Reddit second (build out properly), IG ongoing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's traffic platform hierarchy explicitly. Prioritize: (1) Ensure every real creator has an active Twitter/X account with teaser clips being posted — confirm this is happening and measure click-through to OF. (2) Build a Reddit activation SOP: subreddit research, posting cadence, account warming to avoid bans (§platform-risk overlap). (3) Formally retire the copy-paste trending TikTok Telegram-group method as a primary strategy; replace with the data-driven inspo pipeline Palm already has (§1).

consensus_risers.title: Never mention OnlyFans by name on social — use clean indirect CTAs

variants.claim: Directly referencing OnlyFans on social media platforms (IG, TikTok) risks shadowbans or account termination, so all CTAs must be indirect and discreet.
variants.what_they_do: Instructs operators to keep the social-to-OF funnel link-based but never platform-name the destination in captions, bios phrasing, or spoken content. No five-step click chains either — the funnel is: social content → link → OF page, with the OF brand name omitted from the social layer.

variants.claim: Posting full nudity on X attracts low-spending free-content seekers; tease-only premium content attracts higher-demographic buyers who will pay on OF.
variants.what_they_do: Main account posts only professional photos, good angles, clean aesthetic — no full nudity. Full explicit content is reserved exclusively for the OF vault. The X page functions purely as top-of-funnel tease. No OF link on burner accounts — only on the main. Strategy is: build personality + tease body, never reveal. Explicit quote: 'Do tease, but don't reveal. People follow people, not just bodies.'

sources.quote: do not mention only fans directly on social media because you are going to get banned. Shadowbanned or worse, right? Just don't do it.
Do not post full nudity. Save that for OF. Do tease, but don't reveal.
If you post childish content or you post full nude content, you will pull lower spending fans.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) produces social posts but has no documented compliance rules around OF mention avoidance or shadowban risk on IG/TikTok. This is a platform-risk gap (§ platform-risk) that could silently harm creator accounts.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a compliance rule to Palm's content/posting SOP: no direct OF platform name mentions in captions, audio, or on-screen text on IG or TikTok. Standardize CTA language (e.g., 'link in bio,' 'exclusive content,' 'join my page') across all creator accounts. Include this as a checklist item in the editor approval workflow.

consensus_risers.title: Mimic real girls across stories, highlights and dump-style posts

variants.claim: Social conversion depends on making the page look like an ordinary relatable 'girl next door' by mimicking real women's posting patterns, not glamour content.
variants.what_they_do: Daily story posts plus generic story highlights (food, pets, travel, gym, scenery, often pulled from Pinterest), dump-style multi-photo slideshows with grainy filters and teen-girl audio, spaced/drip-fed over time so the timeline reads as a real account; CTAs framed as 'come say hi' rather than bare links.

variants.claim: Sending creators viral content from models who look like them — same hair color, body type, vibe — dramatically increases the likelihood the creator will replicate it well and that the content will resonate with the right audience.
variants.what_they_do: Agency manually finds a model whose look closely matches the new creator (e.g., 'blonde cheerleader type'), identifies that model's currently-viral videos, and sends them as direct filming references. Creator replicates the format/dance/hook on IG and TikTok.

sources.quote: Seeing your models post day in day out, it creates illusion of a real girl
If you mimic real girls, you can't go wrong
try and find her ideas for Content that are similar to her looking so if she's a blond kind of cheerleader type model then fantastic find another model who looks like her and is currently going viral

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm produces social content via the inspo pipeline (§1 MATURE) but does not codify a 'mimic-real-girl' presence pattern (highlights, story cadence, dump-style) for accounts; real-creator cadence is a gap (§1/§4).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a 'realistic presence' checklist (daily stories, generic highlights, dump-style posts, spaced posting, CTA-not-bare-link) for real-creator IG; directly fills the cadence gap.

consensus_risers.topic: IG ban survivability via satellite account bench
consensus_risers.title: Pre-warm satellite IG accounts as instant replacement bench when main account is banned

variants.claim: Maintaining a pool of continuously warmed satellite accounts means a creator can immediately resume posting on a new account the day their main is banned, with zero cold-start penalty.
variants.what_they_do: Because satellite accounts are already being warmed daily (feed scrolling, posting, engagement), they have account age and trust score built up. When the creator's primary IG account is banned, the agency hands over one or more of these pre-warmed accounts for the creator to continue posting from immediately, avoiding the traffic gap that causes creator churn.

variants.claim: Because AI model accounts are vulnerable to Instagram takedowns, you protect compound growth by keeping the main posting account clean and pushing the monetization link through a separate 'slave' account tagged in the bio.
variants.what_they_do: Main (mother) account posts content normally, builds followers and watch time, and tags a second 'slave' account in bio. The slave account holds the link-in-bio to Fanvue/OF. When Instagram removes the slave account (expected), the mother account survives and keeps its audience; a new slave is swapped in. Real human creators avoid this because they're ID-verified and harder to ban.

sources.quote: if the creators' main accounts do get banned, we have a lot of other accounts which we can just give them, and they can start posting on them, because we've been growing these accounts anyway
we're going to take our models on a second account and then put that in bio
This is the mother account, main account, and then the slave account is where we take it at

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI-persona warmup system (§3/§4) achieves exactly this for synthetic creators — a 90-day warmup produces a ready-to-use account. The same logic is not applied to real-creator backup accounts (§4 🔲 GAP). This is a direct extension of Palm's existing capability to the real-creator line.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Maintain a small bench (3–5) of pre-warmed IG accounts per real creator. Palm's existing warmup operator UI and Publer scheduling could drive this with minimal incremental tooling. Define a handoff SOP: when main account ban is confirmed, assign bench account to creator within 24 hours. This directly reduces model churn from traffic loss (a live pain point per §6 — model retention).

consensus_risers.title: An AI chatbot can replace much of the chat team at a fraction of the cost

variants.claim: A well-configured AI chatting bot can handle 24/7 fan conversations and PPV upsells at a fraction of a chat team's cost, but only if it is carefully set up and trained.
variants.what_they_do: Uses Super Creator's 'Izzy' AI chatbot (~$200-300/month vs a full chatting team), configured with extensive persona data, slang, pricing strategy, and trained on rapport/texting/selling scenarios; warns that a bad setup just creates 'a shitty chatter'.

variants.claim: AI chatbots are improving rapidly and will soon deliver low-latency, high-quality chat at a fraction of the cost of even the cheapest human VAs, making human chatting teams economically unviable.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant flags AI chat as an imminent market force that will undercut human chatters (including cheap Filipino VA teams) on both cost and latency, framing it as a structural shift rather than a distant future trend.

sources.quote: it only costs like $2 $300 a month, which is pennies compared to how much you're forking out... on chatters
if you don't set it up correctly, you are simply going to make a shitty chatter

AI chat bots are making huge improvements very quickly and will soon become a dominant force in the market, delivering low latency, high quality chat at a fraction of the cost of even the cheapest of Filipino virtual assistants

palm_comparison.vs_us: Directly maps to Palm's active AI-chatting project (§8 build-toward): defines a concrete tool pattern and the heavy-configuration requirement.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Evaluate a configurable AI chat bot (Super Creator-class) for Palm's AI-chatting project, budgeting for heavy persona setup/training rather than expecting plug-and-play.

consensus_risers.title: Let AI sort and convert new subs; route spenders to human chatters

variants.claim: The emerging chatting meta is a hybrid where AI handles new-subscriber volume and identifies real spenders, then hands those fans to human chatters for long-term girlfriend-experience retention.
variants.what_they_do: Has AI run all new subscribers and complete the first script to separate spenders from time-wasters, then moves real spenders to a human chatter who gives a daily girlfriend experience to a small client list (~20 fans each); reports ~70% unlock rate and a single whale spending $50k in 4 months.

variants.claim: An AI model trained on past chats can read every chatter's transcripts daily and surface buy-signals, missed offers, and coaching feedback that human supervisors cannot cover at scale.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media pulls Infloww message-board exports into an LLM trained on two years of their own chats to produce daily per-chatter reports (interest/buy signals vs offers sent, left-on-read counts, missed opportunities, KPIs vs peers) because even five supervisors cannot watch 30-40 chatters live.

sources.quote: I will let the AI deal with all the new subscribers... so that it can differentiate between the actual spenders and the time wasters
we want the chatter to specifically just focus on a specific number amount of clients
an AI LLM model that we train... based on two years worth of data of our own chats... how many interest signals... opportunities they missed

palm_comparison.vs_us: Defines the target architecture for Palm's AI-chatting project and future in-house team (§8 build-toward): AI for volume/sorting, humans for whale retention.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design Palm's chat roadmap as a hybrid: AI for new-sub volume and spender-sorting, human chatters owning small VIP client lists for retention, with unlock-rate as a tracked KPI.

consensus_risers.topic: CRM for fan spend tracking to guide PPV pricing
consensus_risers.title: Use a fan CRM (e.g. Inflow) to read spending habits and calibrate per-fan PPV price escalation

variants.claim: A CRM that surfaces each fan's historical spend enables chatters to know exactly how far to push pricing on any individual fan rather than guessing.
variants.what_they_do: The agency uses a CRM tool called 'Inflow' that tracks per-subscriber spending history. Chatters consult this before sending a PPV to decide at what price point to pitch — fans who have spent more get higher asks. The principle is always to move price upward based on observed willingness to pay.

variants.claim: The first action inside a newly-accessed OF account should be sorting existing subscribers by spend to identify whales and avoid wasting chatter time on low-value fans.
variants.what_they_do: Upon first login to the creator's OF account, agency immediately sorts subscribers by total spend, identifying high-value fans to prioritize in DMs and deprioritizing or ignoring low-spend/no-spend subscribers.

sources.quote: if you're using a CRM a customer relationship manager like inflow then those things are going to show you the spending habits for Subs spending habits and then based on these spending habits it shows then you can only go upwards from there
organizing to subscribers seeing who's spent more and who's not organizing them so you don't waste your time

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Whale Hunting tool and Fan Tracker CRM (§9, PARTIAL) that tracks fan data. However, Palm's CRM is primarily a leadership/reporting layer; there is no documented policy for chatters to use fan spend data to calibrate real-time PPV pricing. The external chat team likely does not have structured access to Palm's fan intelligence.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Bridge the §9 fan intelligence system to the external chat team: share per-fan spend summaries so chatters can price escalate based on data rather than guesswork. For the in-house chat roadmap, build a spend-aware pricing prompt directly into the chatter workflow.

consensus_risers.title: Proactively disclose account issues to creators — hiding problems destroys trust faster than the problem itself

variants.claim: Agencies that hide negative account developments lose model trust catastrophically when the model discovers the issue independently; proactive disclosure, even of bad news, strengthens the relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Their agency policy is to give updates on account performance whether good or bad. If something goes wrong (traffic drop, platform issue, content underperformance), they inform the model directly rather than waiting or papering over it. This is presented as a cultural value established from onboarding onwards.

variants.claim: Most creator-agency relationship problems stem from unclear communication about what the agency does and why, not from the tactics themselves.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's top lesson is that agencies fail creators by not explaining the reasoning behind decisions. When a creator complains or pushes back, the wrong response is 'we just do this because it works.' The right response is to genuinely understand what's bothering them — is it the content type? The social media time demand? The lack of transparency? He builds in active listening and clear explanation as a core operational habit, not an ad-hoc fix.

sources.quote: you don't want to just make it seem like everything's going perfectly and them to figure out something's wrong on their own because that's going to be just relationship destroying
a lot of issues that are going to arise are going to be from you not communicating clearly with your client on like what you do and just setting expectations framing stuff the correct way

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator communications policy around bad news or account issues (§6 thin, §9 no retention strategy). The weekly invoice cadence provides a financial signal but there is no SOP for flagging non-financial account problems to creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'bad news protocol' to Palm's creator comms SOP: define what events trigger a proactive creator notification (e.g., >20% subscriber drop, platform flag, traffic source failure) and set a response-time SLA (e.g., notify within 24 hours). Document this protocol and mention it during onboarding so creators know to expect it. Supports §9 retention.

consensus_risers.topic: Creator relationship management and milestone celebration
consensus_risers.title: Assign a dedicated model-facing manager to celebrate creator milestones and prevent churn

variants.claim: Proactively recognizing creator milestones (first viral video, top 1%, highest-earning month) via a dedicated relationship manager — not the CEO — is a primary driver of creator retention and prevents the agency-creator 'butting heads' dynamic.
variants.what_they_do: The speakers describe a specific failure mode: the agency founder misses milestone moments (e.g., creator hits first six figures) because they're buried in ops. The fix is a model-facing manager whose explicit job includes celebrating wins, providing comfortability, and making the creator feel involved in the agency. Examples given: 'you just hit the top 1%,' 'your highest month ever,' 'first 1M video.' This person also handles weekly or daily content feedback, direction relays, and expectation-setting — keeping creators informed and emotionally invested in the partnership.

variants.claim: At premium price points, agencies can and should offer direct human communication — calls, responsive emails, real answers — which becomes the actual differentiator against lower-priced automated competitors.
variants.what_they_do: Explicitly offers phone calls, responsive emails, and real conversations with clients as part of service delivery. Frames this as only economically viable at $1,500–$2,500+/month (can't afford it at $97). Positions human accessibility as the competitive moat against SaaS/AI commodity offerings.

sources.quote: highlighting those Milestones is big for sure like hey let's say you have a new Creator right you tell her hey we just hit the top 1% like congrats or like let's say her she just hit her highest month ever
we're going to be normal human beings. That's right. We're going to call them. We're going to send them emails. We're going to answer their questions.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator retention strategy or milestone-tracking cadence (§9 gap). Whale-hunting and Fan Tracker CRM exist for fan-side retention but not creator-side. Creator communication runs through Josh directly today (§10), which doesn't scale and leaves milestone moments to chance. No win-back or churn-signal framework exists.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As the model-manager role is built out (see delegation finding above), explicitly include milestone-celebration and retention cadence in the job spec: weekly check-in call or message, milestone alerts triggered from revenue dashboard (e.g., Airtable rule when monthly earnings hit new high), and a standard 'congrats' communication template. Add a creator-churn-risk signal to the Airtable Creators table (e.g., days since last communication, revenue trend direction) to surface at-risk creators proactively.

consensus_risers.title: Target mid-tier creators (10K followers, ~$1-3K/mo) not top earners

variants.claim: Beginner agencies should target creators with ~10-15K followers already making $1-3K/month rather than chasing large verified accounts who won't respond.
variants.what_they_do: Explicitly filter outreach to creators in the 10K-15K follower range earning roughly $1-3K/month on OF, on the basis that they are more open to support, more reachable via DM, and more likely to convert to a managed relationship.

variants.claim: Agencies should segment potential creator clients into four revenue bands and target the tier where their service delivers the highest ROI.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan segments OF creators into four bands: $0–10K/mo, $10–50K/mo, $100–500K/mo, and $500K+/mo. New/smaller agencies are directed to target the $0–10K band because those creators get the biggest ROI from management services. He sends clients a segmentation sheet and has them identify which band to focus on exclusively, so outreach and pitching is concentrated rather than scattered.

sources.quote: targeting more beginner friendly creators as well. Also, someone that's kind of starting out, 10,000 15,000 followers, ones that are also making around 10K a month or so

we split there up into four market segments right only fans models making 500k a month only fans models making between 100 500k a month only fans models making 10 to 50K a month and only fans models anything from 0 to 10K

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented targeting criteria (§6 GAP). No internal baseline exists for follower count, current earnings, or engagement thresholds that qualify a creator for outreach.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a minimum viable creator profile for outreach: e.g., 10K-20K IG followers, $1-3K/mo existing OF revenue, active posting cadence. Add these as qualification fields to the creator onboarding form or a pre-onboarding screening step in Airtable.

consensus_risers.topic: Creator recruiting as a permanent, dedicated department
consensus_risers.title: Treat creator recruiting as a full internal department that never stops, not an ad-hoc activity

variants.claim: Recruiting is the primary scaling lever for an OFM agency because creator quality and quantity directly determine revenue ceiling, yet most agencies treat it as an afterthought rather than a dedicated, always-on department.
variants.what_they_do: Agency runs recruiting as a named department with dedicated effort and headcount, not delegated to the CEO's spare time. The principle is that the ideal creator (great look, great work ethic, fully cooperative) effectively makes the agency's systems and marketing effort secondary — and that five such creators could produce $200–300k/month. Recruiting never pauses regardless of current creator count.

variants.claim: The single biggest lever that broke Ellis past $30K/month was an acquisition system that never stops, not better marketing or chatting.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis runs a three-component acquisition machine (lead-magnet content + paid ads + a specific outreach method) combined with VSLs, a funnel, a call-setting service, and a closer — all running simultaneously and never paused even when onboarding a new creator. The system converts models continuously without founder involvement.

sources.quote: Your recruiting shouldn't stop. Your recruiting shouldn't be left on the side. It's a full department in your agency that if you don't take it seriously, you're always going to be stuck.
the only thing that took us past it, it wasn't marketing, it wasn't chatting, it was getting a solid acquisition system that never stops running

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is currently Josh-DM-led with no systematic machine (§6 — THIN). The 75-conversation market research shows intentionality, but there is no documented recruiting SOP, dedicated headcount, or outbound funnel for creators. This is the agency's most documented GAP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Prioritise building a creator acquisition system as a named workstream: define an ideal creator profile (look, niche, work ethic, platform readiness), document an outbound DM sequence and qualification rubric, and assign or hire someone to run it full-time as volume scales. Track pipeline metrics (outreach sent, response rate, signed rate) in Airtable. Do not pause recruiting when the current roster feels full.

consensus_risers.topic: Agency value proposition positioning for creator signing
consensus_risers.title: Lead creator pitches with organic social capability, not chat or back-end ops

variants.claim: The value proposition that closes creator deals in 2026 is demonstrable front-end marketing capability — organic social reach and traffic generation — not chatting or business infrastructure.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant advises that agencies pitching creators should lead with their organic social marketing system and track record of growing audiences, since that is what creators cannot do themselves and cannot easily get elsewhere at differentiated quality.

variants.claim: Stating '50% revenue split' is not a real offer — agencies must articulate specific outcomes (more time, more money, viral growth) to differentiate, because every competitor leads with the same split.
variants.what_they_do: Agency frames its pitch around two core creator pain points: (1) inability to grow new fans and (2) lack of time to manage everything. The offer is sold as 'save your time, make more money' with concrete evidence (screenshots of revenue growth from existing clients). The 50% figure is justified by showing the creator went from $8K to $108K/month — the split is irrelevant when absolute earnings quadruple.

sources.quote: the greatest value you can possibly offer a new model is on the front end. Using organic social to create marketing campaigns and distribution networks that generate massive volumes of high quality traffic
what not to do is... my agency takes 50% blah blah. This is not a real offer. It is instead a revenue split. There is no value to that

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition system is Josh-DM-led with no systematic pitch framework documented (§6 GAP). It is unknown whether current pitches lead with Palm's organic social strength. Given Palm's mature content engine (§1), this capability should be front-and-center in every creator conversation but is not yet formalized.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a formal creator pitch deck or one-pager that leads with Palm's organic social system: data-driven inspo sourcing, AI-assisted analysis, and proven posting infrastructure. Include any growth metrics from existing creators. This directly addresses the §6 GAP in systematic acquisition and gives Josh a repeatable, differentiating pitch rather than ad-hoc DM conversations.

consensus_risers.title: New agencies should master one creator before signing multiple

variants.claim: Beginners who try to sign many clients simultaneously disappoint everyone and burn out; mastering operations on one client first produces better outcomes.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan explicitly advises new agency operators to sign a single creator, test all marketing and chatting strategies on that one account, and only expand once they know what works. He argues that without in-house chatters (which beginners can't afford) and without proven marketing, having five-plus clients is unmanageable and leads to poor results across the board.

variants.claim: New agencies should aim to sign two clients simultaneously: one as a hedge if the other underperforms, but no more than two to avoid operational overwhelm before systems are proven.
variants.what_they_do: Prescribes a deliberate two-client start: if one relationship fails the other provides continuity, while capping at two prevents the operator from over-extending before they have SOPs, chatters, or reliable traffic in place.

sources.quote: focus on one client to begin with because a lot of beginners will try and just like scatter gun and try and get as many clients as they can thinking that that is going to solve their problems
you should ideally aim to sign two clients from the get-go. If you just have one, you can stay with one, of course, but in most cases, one of them might not work out

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition approach is Josh-DM-led without a documented onboarding capacity limit (§6). There is no written rule about how many creators Palm takes on relative to operational readiness (chatting outsourced §8, thin content vault §2). This principle is implicitly understood but not codified as a scaling gate.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define and document Palm's current operational capacity ceiling per creator tier — e.g., how many creators can be served well given current editor headcount, outsourced chat team bandwidth, and social pipeline throughput. Use this as a hard gate in the acquisition process so new signings only happen when capacity exists, preventing the quality dilution Dylan describes.

consensus_risers.topic: Content repurposing — structural uniqueness to evade IG detection
consensus_risers.title: Make repurposed Reels structurally different, not just color-adjusted

variants.claim: Changing only brightness/contrast is insufficient to fool Instagram's duplicate-detection; the video's structure must be genuinely altered.
variants.what_they_do: They download the original Reel watermark-free (SnapTick/SnapInsta), then apply structural edits in any editing app: trim 0.1s from a scene cut, mirror the clip, insert a different opening scene, and also adjust color/contrast slightly — the goal is a video that IS different, not merely looks different.

variants.claim: AI-generated media carries hidden metadata that platforms read to tag/throttle content, so removing it before posting materially restores reach.
variants.what_they_do: Removes AI metadata from generated images/videos with a free tool or re-export before posting; reports reach went up ~40% and videos hit explore again after they started doing this.

sources.quote: you're going to take 0.1 second out of one scene. You're going to basically mirror the video, right? You're going to add a different scene at the beginning
there's a metadata attached to those files that says AI

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) produces organic, creator-filmed Reels and does not currently have a repurposing workflow. The structural-edit technique is a distinct add-on to the existing pipeline for any scaled reposting layer.
palm_comparison.recommendation: If Palm ever runs multi-account reposting for a single creator, document a mandatory structural-edit checklist (scene trim, mirror, new intro scene + minor color grade) before export. Add this as a step in the existing pipeline for repurposed variants, distinct from the organic primary post.

consensus_risers.title: Treat every revenue-generating asset as likely to disappear tomorrow; diversify across creators, accounts, and traffic sources

variants.claim: Because Instagram accounts, OF accounts, and individual creators can all disappear overnight without warning, any agency with revenue concentrated in a single asset is one ban or personal decision away from zero.
variants.what_they_do: Agency explicitly flags concentration risk as a daily operational concern: monitors the revenue share of each creator, each Instagram account, and each employee. If one creator generates the majority of revenue, the agency treats that as a red flag and accelerates recruiting to dilute the dependency. Same logic applied to Instagram accounts (build more traffic sources, have a ban-response protocol) and key employees (have them document and train backups before they leave).

variants.claim: Assuming a baseline account-ban burn rate and pre-provisioning reserve accounts is required to run social acquisition at scale without interruption.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media issues 200 accounts per platform per model (about 100 active immediately, 100 in reserve), runs a daily script using public profile views to auto-detect bans, and accepts that even fully organic operations always carry some burn.

sources.quote: You have one creator that makes the majority of the revenue, that should be a fucking red flag. That should scare the hell out of you.
100 that become active immediately when they start then 100 for reserve... there's always the risk of having some bans

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI persona infrastructure already provides meaningful diversification (multiple accounts, hardware-isolated, §3/§4 — MATURE). However, on the real-creator side, creator concentration risk is not formally tracked (§9 — PARTIAL; §7 — GAP). There is no documented ban-response protocol or creator-departure contingency plan in the codebase.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'revenue concentration' metric to the agency health dashboard: flag any single creator, IG account, or traffic channel that exceeds 40% of total revenue. For the top-concentration creator, document a written contingency plan (which backup account takes traffic, who covers DMs, how fast can recruiting replace volume). For IG accounts, formalise the ban-response protocol currently referenced but not codified.

new_evidence.title: Warm fans with a slightly discounted first PPV, then escalate price on every subsequent send

variants.claim: The first PPV sale is a warm-up purchase at a slight discount; once a fan buys it, every future PPV should be priced at the floor or higher, escalating as the conversation progresses.
variants.what_they_do: New subscribers receive an automated script that pitches a first video at $34.99 (slightly below the $39.99 floor) to reduce friction and get a first purchase. After that purchase the fan is considered 'warmed up' and all future content is priced at $40+ for video and $20+ for images, escalating upward as the sexting session progresses. The escalation is tied to emotional momentum — the longer the conversation runs, the more the fan will pay.

variants.claim: Pricing the first PPV low (roughly $5-$20) exists to get the fan used to spending, not to make money on that sale.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard caps the first PPV at $5-$20, never higher, to get the customer used to the habit of buying on the platform before laddering up; habibi tests a brand-new buyer with a small first buy (e.g. $9) to read his boundaries.

variants.claim: The first PPV sale should always be $5 or less to condition the subscriber to buy; subsequent sales increase in price incrementally, and the overall set is structured as 7 separate sales attempts at progressively higher price points.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to structure one content 'set' as 7 distinct sales opportunities (individual pics, pic+video combos, etc.) with prices rising at each step. The first sale is always ≤$5 — not because the content is cheap, but to make the buy frictionless and establish a Pavlovian habit: sub sees PPV → sub unlocks. Subs who won't spend $5 are deprioritized or ignored. New chatters are taught to use small price increments (e.g., $5 → $7 → $10) rather than jumping to $25+. Selling too much too soon is explicitly called out as a sub-burning mistake; longevity and lifetime value are the stated goal.

variants.claim: Starting the PPV ladder at a low price ($15) minimizes the psychological barrier to the first purchase, making it far more likely the subscriber enters the escalation funnel and ultimately spends $300–$500.
variants.what_they_do: The specific ladder cited: $15 → $30 → $60 → $120 → $200 → $200. Entry price is low enough to feel trivial. Each step roughly doubles, creating anchoring where each subsequent price feels proportional to the last rather than shocking. The final $200 items are 'full action' — maximum explicitness justifying maximum price. Total session value: ~$625 at full conversion.

variants.claim: Sending low-ticket PPVs ($15–$20) first gauges a subscriber's willingness to spend and builds purchasing habit before high-ticket ($80–$100+) asks.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter begins by offering content in the $15–$20 range ('dipping a toe in the water'). After multiple purchases at this level, the chatter reads the subscriber's comfort and escalates to high-ticket PPVs ($80, $90, $100+). No high-ticket offer is sent cold to a new subscriber.

variants.claim: A soft verbal commitment ('yes train') before sending the first PPV — combined with a $15–$25 price point and partial-nudity content — significantly increases buy-through rates.
variants.what_they_do: Before sending any PPV, the chatter runs a 'yes train': a question that sounds like consent ('if I do this, do you promise you'll go easy on me?') but functions as a purchase pre-commitment. When the fan says yes, he feels psychologically obligated to buy the PPV that follows. First PPV is priced $15–$25 (higher if the model is positioned as premium; lower if marketed with explicit free content). Content is partial nudity only (top off). Subsequent PPVs escalate: bottoms off → full nude → solo play. Between each PPV, the chatter rebuilds arousal with free teasers and descriptive text before the next ask. The session ends when the fan signals spending fatigue — at which point no further PPV is pushed.

variants.claim: Selling via an escalating price ladder rather than one high ask raises total spend while keeping each step psychologically easy.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard runs a default ladder of 15, 25, 45, 65, then 100-120, and warns that hitting a warmed-up fan with a single $80 PPV makes him leave for no money, where the same total is extracted reliably in small rising steps.

variants.claim: Monetize via a scripted sequence where each stage is more explicit and priced higher than the last, rather than flat pricing.
variants.what_they_do: A 'script' is pre-made content starting safe-for-work and escalating; price rises each stage, e.g. ~$10-15, then ~$35, ~$70, ~$140, ~$200. Between paid stages, send free photos at the level the fan already unlocked to over-deliver and stand out from other creators.

new_evidence.title: Never link OF/Fanvue directly; warm accounts before linking

variants.claim: To avoid bans, operators never post adult-platform links directly on social and warm new accounts before adding any link-in-bio CTA.
variants.what_they_do: Always route via a neutral landing page (e.g. Get All My Links) since a reported direct OF/Fanvue link can trigger strikes/bans; spend the first ~30 days on profile setup, spaced posting and trust-score building before putting links in bio; avoid rapid-burst follows/comments and automation that trip platform limits.

variants.claim: New social accounts should be warmed as normal users and post content for about two weeks before adding the OnlyFans link to protect trust score.
variants.what_they_do: Acts as a normal user (scroll/like, follow niche on day 2, don't post day 1), waits ~2 weeks of posting before adding any link, and pre-builds accounts before signing a client so revenue never stalls.

variants.claim: Instagram bans in OFM are driven mainly by unfocused overtly-sexual content and low trust score, not by IP/proxy/link setup as most operators assume.
variants.what_they_do: After testing 50+ accounts, concludes content (half-naked, unbranded, unfocused) is the highest-weight ban trigger; uses normal iPhone + real SIM/hotspot, no VPN/proxy, and builds a branded persona instead of bikini spam.

warm up slowly, act like a human

90% of bans initiate due to your content having zero focus

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI warmup is mature with a 90-day cadence, Day-21 link handoff and Day-45 OF-CTA gate (§4), but real-creator warmup and link-safety rules are a gap (§4 THIN).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a real-creator account-warmup plus landing-page-only link policy mirroring the AI warmup gates (§4) to reduce strike/ban risk on creator socials.

new_evidence.topic: Reddit LTV vs. other traffic sources
new_evidence.title: Reddit organic NSFW traffic produces the highest fan LTV of any channel — $8–$35 on free pages

variants.claim: Fans acquired through niche-matched NSFW Reddit posts have significantly higher LTV than Instagram or dating-app traffic because they self-selected into a subreddit specifically for their fetish and actively chose to view the content, indicating high purchase intent.
variants.what_they_do: Agency benchmarks LTV by traffic source. Reddit organic NSFW: $8–$35 LTV on free pages. Dating app traffic also historically high LTV. Instagram traffic lower. Agency prioritises Reddit despite declining ROI trend because subscriber quality (intentionality) remains superior. At peak (3 years ago) one viral SFW post could drive 3,000–5,000 subs/day; currently realistic target is 10+ paid subs/day per account on a well-matched creator.

new_evidence.title: PPV Anchors: Plant a story or fantasy 6–24 hours before sending the PPV to manufacture perceived relevance

The delay is what makes it feel authentic rather than scripted. Feed-level anchors (outfit post, emotional story post) can hit many subs simultaneously, with chatters following up individually in DMs.

When the sub is clearly engaged, the chatter pitches the PPV: 'I made this video thinking about doing that for the first time.' They connect specific moments in the video to the sexting ('at 2:41 I was thinking about what you said about…'). Fantasies are ranked from least to most explicit; each PPV in the loop uses the next fantasy up the ladder. This gives chatters an inexhaustible, varied pitch mechanism that doesn't repeat.
